topics.title: Implement Basic Search Strategies - 8-Puzzle Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL507 - AI ALGORITHM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement Basic Search Strategies - 8-Puzzle Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **8-Puzzle Problem** is a classical *uninformed (blind) search* benchmark in Artificial Intelligence, consisting of a **3 × 3 grid** containing eight numbered, movable tiles (1–8) and **one blank space** (denoted by 0). The puzzle is in a *randomized initial state*, and the agent must apply a sequence of legal moves (sliding an adjacent tile into the blank space) to reach a **specified goal configuration**, typically:

$$
S_{goal} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Formally, the problem is defined as a 5-tuple:

$$
P = \langle S, S_0, A, T, G \rangle
$$

Where:
- $S$ — finite set of states (state space)
- $S_0$ — initial state
- $A$ — set of actions (Up, Down, Left, Right)
- $T$ — transition function $T: S \times A \rightarrow S$
- $G$ — set of goal states

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8-puzzle is the canonical teaching vehicle for **uninformed search strategies** — BFS, DFS, Uniform-Cost Search (UCS), Depth-Limited Search (DLS), and Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS). Mastery of state representation, move generation, and goal testing is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **wooden sliding-tile toy** your grandparents owned. You can only push one tile at a time into the adjacent empty slot — never lift a tile or rearrange them by hand. Your task: arrange numbers in order by only sliding.

In AI terms:
- The **board configuration** = a *state*
- The **blank position** = where an action can be applied
- A **sliding move** = a *successor function* generating child states
- **Reaching the ordered configuration** = satisfying the *goal test*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 8-Puzzle Matters in AI:**
> 1. The state space is large enough to be non-trivial: **9! / 2 = 181,440 reachable states** (divided by 2 because half the permutations are unreachable due to parity).
> 2. The branching factor is small (2 to 4), making exhaustive searches feasible for shallow depths but computationally explosive at deeper depths.
> 3. It is a *rearrangement problem* representative of real-world puzzles like Rubik's cube, sliding robots, and route planning.

## 1.3 State Representation in Code

The most common encoding is a **tuple of 9 integers** read row-major:

$$
s = (t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4, t_5, t_6, t_7, t_8, t_9), \quad t_i \in \{0,1,\dots,8\}
$$

This immutable representation guarantees hashability (essential for `visited` sets) and supports fast comparison.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Search Algorithm

Every search strategy must implement the following five logical steps:

1. **State Initialization:** Create the root node from $S_0$.
2. **Frontier Management:** Maintain a data structure (queue/stack/priority-queue) holding nodes awaiting expansion.
3. **Goal Test:** Check whether the current node's state belongs to $G$.
4. **Successor Generation:** Apply each legal action $a \in A$ to produce child nodes.
5. **Visited Tracking:** Maintain a *closed set* to prevent revisiting states (graph search vs. tree search).

> [!TIP]
> **Tree Search vs. Graph Search:**
> - *Tree Search*: Allows revisiting states → can loop infinitely on cyclic state spaces.
> - *Graph Search*: Uses a `visited` set → guarantees termination on finite graphs but requires $\mathcal{O}(\vert S \vert)$ memory.

## 2.2 The Five Uninformed Search Strategies

| Strategy | Data Structure | Optimal? | Complete? | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** | FIFO Queue | Yes (unit cost) | Yes | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ |
| **Depth-First Search (DFS)** | LIFO Stack | No | No (infinite spaces) | $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$ |
| **Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)** | Priority Queue (by $g(n)$) | Yes | Yes (positive costs) | $\mathcal{O}(b^{\lfloor C^{*} / \epsilon \rfloor})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^{\lfloor C^{*} / \epsilon \rfloor})$ |
| **Depth-Limited Search (DLS)** | LIFO Stack + limit $\ell$ | No | No (if $\ell < d$) | $\mathcal{O}(b^{\ell})$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot \ell)$ |
| **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)** | Repeated DLS, $\ell = 0,1,2\dots$ | Yes (unit cost) | Yes | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend for the Formula Sheet:**
> - $b$ = branching factor (avg. $\approx 2.67$ for 8-puzzle at non-corner blank positions)
> - $d$ = depth of the shallowest goal
> - $m$ = maximum depth of the search tree
> - $C^{*}$ = cost of optimal solution
> - $\epsilon$ = minimum step cost

## 2.3 Why Avoid Pipes (`|`) in KTU Markdown Tables

KTU's automated PDF/Markdown converters occasionally misparse vertical pipes inside math expressions like $\vert x \vert$ as column delimiters. **Always** use `\mid` or `\vert` inside `$$` math blocks to preserve table integrity.

## 2.4 Practical Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application Analogue of 8-Puzzle Search |
|---|---|
| **Robotics** | Path planning for tile-rearrangement manipulators |
| **Logistics** | Container reshuffling in warehouse loading bays |
| **VLSI Design** | Block placement problems in chip floor-planning |
| **Game AI** | Solitaire/Tic-Tac-Toe state-space exploration |
| **Bioinformatics** | Genome rearrangement by translocation operators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The transition from "puzzle" to "search problem" is the single most important conceptual leap. Whenever you see *uninformed search* in the question paper, the answer must emphasize (a) the frontier data structure and (b) the completeness/optimality trade-off.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Branching Factor of the 8-Puzzle

The 8-puzzle does **not** have a uniform branching factor — it depends on the position of the blank tile.

**Step 1:** Identify the blank position $(r, c)$ in the 3 × 3 grid.

**Step 2:** Count the legal moves. A corner has 2 neighbors, an edge has 3, and the center has 4:

$$
b(r, c) = \begin{cases}
2, & \text{if } (r,c) \in \{(0,0), (0,2), (2,0), (2,2)\} \\
3, & \text{if } (r,c) \in \{(0,1), (1,0), (1,2), (2,1)\} \\
4, & \text{if } (r,c) = (1,1)
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the *average branching factor* by weighting each region:

$$
\bar{b} = \frac{4 \cdot 2 + 4 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 4}{9} = \frac{8 + 12 + 4}{9} = \frac{24}{9} \approx 2.67
$$

This value $\bar{b} \approx 2.67$ is used in textbook complexity estimates.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Reachable State Count

**Step 1:** A 3 × 3 board with 9 distinct tiles has $9! = 362{,}880$ permutations.

**Step 2:** Half are unreachable due to *parity invariant*. Any legal move is a transposition of the blank with an adjacent tile, which toggles the parity of the permutation.

**Step 3:** Therefore reachable states:

$$
\mid S \mid = \frac{9!}{2} = \frac{362{,}880}{2} = 181{,}440
$$

## 3.3 Heuristic Estimations (Foundations for A* — Future Modules)

Although Module 2 focuses on **uninformed** search, examiners often introduce heuristic functions as motivation. Two foundational heuristics:

### 3.3.1 Misplaced Tiles Heuristic

$$
h_1(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \mathbb{1}_{\left[ t_i^{(n)} \neq t_i^{(goal)} \right]}
$$

**Worked example** for state $(2, 8, 3, 1, 6, 4, 7, 0, 5)$ vs. goal $(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1 &= [2\neq1] + [8\neq2] + [3=3] + [1\neq4] + [6\neq5] + [4\neq6] + [7=7] + [0\neq8] \\
&= 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 1 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Manhattan Distance Heuristic

$$
h_2(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{8} \left( \mid r_i^{(n)} - r_i^{(goal)} \mid + \mid c_i^{(n)} - c_i^{(goal)} \mid \right)
$$

**Worked example** for tile **8** at position $(0,1)$ in goal $(2,1)$:

$$
d_{8} = \mid 0 - 2 \mid + \mid 1 - 1 \mid = 2 + 0 = 2
$$

Repeating for all tiles and summing yields the total Manhattan distance.

## 3.4 Worked Trace: BFS on a Simplified 8-Puzzle

Initial state: $(1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 5, 6, 7, 8)$, goal: $(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0)$.

| Step | Frontier (FIFO order) | Visited Set | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $[N_0]$ | $\{(1,2,3,4,0,5,6,7,8)\}$ | Initialize root |
| 1 | $[N_1, N_2, N_3]$ | adds 3 successors | Expand $N_0$ |
| 2 | $[N_2, N_3, N_4, N_5]$ | adds 2 new | Pop $N_1$, expand |
| 3 | $[N_3, N_4, N_5, \dots]$ | … | Continue |
| ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ |
| k | contains $N_{goal}$ | complete | **Solution found at depth k** |

BFS finds the shortest path (depth-first) but consumes $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Selection Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the decision logic for choosing an appropriate uninformed search strategy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: 8-Puzzle Defined] --> nodeB{Step Costs Uniform?}
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeC{Solution Depth Known?}
    nodeB -- No --> nodeD[Use Uniform Cost Search UCS]
    nodeC -- Yes Small --> nodeE[Use Breadth First Search BFS]
    nodeC -- Yes Large --> nodeF[Use Iterative Deepening DFS]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeG{Memory Constrained?}
    nodeG -- Yes --> nodeH[Use Depth Limited Search]
    nodeG -- No --> nodeI[Use Standard DFS]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Goal Found: Return Path]
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[End]
```

## 4.2 State Expansion Diagram (Move Operators)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[State: Blank at Row 0 Col 1] --> nodeA1[Action Up Invalid]
    nodeS1 --> nodeA2[Action Down: Swap with Row 1 Col 1]
    nodeS1 --> nodeA3[Action Left: Swap with Row 0 Col 0]
    nodeS1 --> nodeA4[Action Right: Swap with Row 0 Col 2]
    nodeA2 --> nodeS2[Child State 1]
    nodeA3 --> nodeS3[Child State 2]
    nodeA4 --> nodeS4[Child State 3]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Graph Search Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeInput[Input: Initial State and Goal State] --> nodeInit[Initialize Root Node]
    nodeInit --> nodeFrontier[Frontier Data Structure]
    nodeFrontier --> nodePop{Pop Node}
    nodePop --> nodeGoalTest{Goal Test}
    nodeGoalTest -- Yes --> nodeReturn[Reconstruct Path via Parent Pointers]
    nodeGoalTest -- No --> nodeVisited{In Visited Set?}
    nodeVisited -- Yes --> nodePop
    nodeVisited -- No --> nodeAdd[Add to Visited]
    nodeAdd --> nodeExpand[Generate Successors via Move Operators]
    nodeExpand --> nodeFrontier
    nodeReturn --> nodeOutput[Output: Sequence of Moves]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Strategy Trade-Off (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBFS[BF S Guarantees Optimality] --> nodeCostBFS[Cost: High Memory O B to D]
    nodeDFS[DFS Uses Low Memory] --> nodeRiskDFS[Risk: May Miss Optimal Path]
    nodeUCS[UCS Handles Variable Costs] --> nodeCostUCS[Cost: Priority Queue Overhead]
    nodeIDDFS[IDDFS Combines BFS Optimality] --> nodeBenefitID[Benefit: DFS Memory Efficiency]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a logical stage of the search engine. For the KTU lab exam, you may be asked to draw or describe this architecture in 2–3 marks. Memorize the **frontier type** for each strategy — it is the most commonly tested item.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q: Define the 8-puzzle problem. List any two uninformed search strategies that can solve it.**

**Model Answer:**
The 8-puzzle problem is a 3 × 3 sliding-tile puzzle with eight numbered tiles and one blank space, where the goal is to reach an ordered configuration from an arbitrary starting state by sliding tiles into the blank. Each state has a branching factor of 2 to 4 depending on the blank's position.

Two uninformed strategies:
1. **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** — uses a FIFO queue, guarantees optimal solution for uniform step costs.
2. **Depth-First Search (DFS)** — uses a LIFO stack, requires less memory but may not find the optimal solution.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q: Why is Iterative Deepening DFS preferred over plain BFS for deep state spaces like the 8-puzzle?**

**Model Answer:**
IDDFS combines the **memory efficiency of DFS** ($\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$) with the **optimality of BFS** (finds the shallowest goal). For the 8-puzzle, BFS would require storing $\mathcal{O}(b^d) \approx 2.67^d$ nodes, which grows exponentially. IDDFS re-runs depth-limited DFS with increasing limits $0, 1, 2, \dots, d$, reusing memory at each iteration and achieving $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ time but only $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ space.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]
>
> **(a)** Explain the **state space representation**, **successor function**, and **goal test** for the 8-puzzle problem. Show how moves Up, Down, Left, Right are generated from a given blank position. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Implement **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** in Python to solve an 8-puzzle instance. Trace its execution on the initial state $(1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 5, 6, 7, 8)$ with goal $(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0)$. Comment on its time and space complexity. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

- **State Representation:** A tuple of 9 integers $(t_0, t_1, \dots, t_8)$ in row-major order. The blank is encoded as `0`.
- **Successor Function:** Given blank at index $i$:
  - **Up:** valid if $i \geq 3$. Swap blank with tile at index $i - 3$.
  - **Down:** valid if $i \leq 5$. Swap blank with tile at index $i + 3$.
  - **Left:** valid if $i \notin \{0, 3, 6\}$. Swap blank with tile at index $i - 1$.
  - **Right:** valid if $i \notin \{2, 5, 8\}$. Swap blank with tile at index $i + 1$.
- **Goal Test:** Return `True` if `state == (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0)`.

**Model Solution (b) — Python Implementation:**

```python
from collections import deque

GOAL = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0)

def get_neighbors(state):
    neighbors = []
    i = state.index(0)              # blank index
    row, col = divmod(i, 3)
    moves = [("Up", -3), ("Down", 3), ("Left", -1), ("Right", 1)]
    for name, delta in moves:
        j = i + delta
        if 0 <= j < 9 and not (name == "Left" and col == 0) \
                        and not (name == "Right" and col == 2):
            new_state = list(state)
            new_state[i], new_state[j] = new_state[j], new_state[i]
            neighbors.append((name, tuple(new_state)))
    return neighbors

def bfs(initial):
    frontier = deque([(initial, [])])
    visited = {initial}
    while frontier:
        state, path = frontier.popleft()
        if state == GOAL:
            return path
        for action, child in get_neighbors(state):
            if child not in visited:
                visited.add(child)
                frontier.append((child, path + [action]))
    return None

print(bfs((1, 2, 3, 4, 0, 5, 6, 7, 8)))
```

**Trace on the given instance:** The blank starts at index 4 (center). BFS expands level by level: depth 0 → root; depth 1 → 4 children; depth 2 → 8 grandchildren. The shallowest goal is at depth 1 via the action "Down". Output: `['Down']`.

**Complexity:** Time $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ and space $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ where $d=1$, $b\approx 2.67$.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
>
> **(a)** Compare **DFS, BFS, and IDDFS** for solving the 8-puzzle. Prepare a comparative table covering frontier data structure, completeness, optimality, time, and space complexity. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Implement **Iterative Deepening DFS** and show its execution trace for an 8-puzzle instance requiring a solution at depth 3. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

| Property | BFS | DFS | IDDFS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frontier | FIFO Queue | LIFO Stack | Repeated DLS |
| Complete? | Yes | No | Yes |
| Optimal? | Yes (unit cost) | No | Yes (unit cost) |
| Time | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^m)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ |
| Space | $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$ | $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot d)$ |

**Model Solution (b) — IDDFS Implementation:**

```python
def dls(state, limit, path):
    if state == GOAL:
        return path
    if limit == 0:
        return None
    for action, child in get_neighbors(state):
        if child not in path:
            result = dls(child, limit - 1, path + [child])
            if result is not None:
                return [action] + result
    return None

def iddfs(initial, max_depth=20):
    for depth in range(max_depth + 1):
        result = dls(initial, depth, [initial])
        if result is not None:
            return result
    return None
```

**Execution Trace (depth = 3):**
- Iteration 0: DLS limit 0 — no goal.
- Iteration 1: DLS limit 1 — expand root only.
- Iteration 2: DLS limit 2 — explore 2 levels.
- Iteration 3: DLS limit 3 — goal found, return path of length 3.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting parity check:** If you provide an unreachable initial state (e.g., a permutation with wrong parity), your BFS will exhaust the entire state space of 181,440 nodes and return `None`. Examiners award **0 marks** if you do not justify the unreachable status.
> 2. **Using mutable lists as dictionary keys:** Hashing fails on lists. Always convert to `tuple` before adding to `visited`.
> 3. **Not tracking parent pointers:** If your BFS returns only the goal node without reconstructing the path, you lose 3–4 marks. Always maintain a `parent` dictionary or extend the path list.
> 4. **Confusing tree-search DFS with graph-search DFS:** Graph search is mandatory for the 8-puzzle to prevent cycles; tree search can run forever. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The 8-puzzle has **9! / 2 = 181,440** reachable states due to parity invariance.
- The **average branching factor** is $\bar{b} \approx 2.67$, derived from corner-edge-center counts.
- **BFS** is optimal and complete but memory-heavy: $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$.
- **DFS** is memory-efficient: $\mathcal{O}(b \cdot m)$, but incomplete and non-optimal.
- **UCS** is optimal for variable step costs and uses a priority queue keyed on $g(n)$.
- **DLS** bounds recursion at depth $\ell$, guaranteeing termination in finite trees.
- **IDDFS** is the *gold standard* for unknown-depth search: combines BFS optimality with DFS memory profile.
- **State representation** must use immutable types (tuples) to enable hashing in `visited` sets.
- **Move generation** must respect grid boundaries (avoid index out-of-range errors).
- **Goal test** is constant-time comparison against the canonical goal tuple $(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0)$.
- **Heuristics** ($h_1$ Misplaced Tiles, $h_2$ Manhattan Distance) are *informed* search additions — not required for uninformed Module 2, but conceptually bridge to A*.
- Always mention **graph search vs. tree search** when justifying completeness claims.
- **Parent pointer reconstruction** is essential for any KTU viva or practical exam on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement Variants of Hill-Climbing and Genetic Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL507 - AI ALGORITHM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement Variants of Hill-Climbing and Genetic Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hill Climbing — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hill Climbing (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> *Hill Climbing is an Informed Search / Local Search technique in Artificial Intelligence that iteratively moves from the current state to a neighbouring state that has a higher value of an evaluation (heuristic) function, terminating when no neighbour offers an improvement or a pre-defined goal state is reached.*

In KTU 2024 terminology, hill climbing belongs to the family of **Local Search Algorithms** and is classified under **Informed Search Heuristics** because it uses a heuristic function $h(n)$ to evaluate how "good" a state is. The goal is to **maximize** $h(n)$ for a maximization problem (or **minimize** it in case of a cost formulation).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Blind Mountain Climber

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Foggy Mountain Climb**
> Imagine you are standing on a foggy mountain in the dark. You cannot see the summit. The only thing you can do is **feel with your foot** — wherever the ground rises steepest under your foot, you take a step in that direction. You keep doing this until, in every direction you step, the ground either stays flat or goes down. At that point, you stop. You may be at the true peak — or you may be stuck on a small hill (a *local maximum*). This is exactly what Hill Climbing does in the state-space landscape.

**Core Terminology Used by KTU Examiners:**
- **State Space Landscape** — graph of all possible states with height = heuristic value
- **Local Maximum** — a state better than all its neighbours but not the global best
- **Global Maximum** — the absolute best state in the entire state space
- **Plateau** — a flat region of the landscape where neighbours have equal values
- **Ridge** — a sequence of local maxima that is hard to traverse
- **Heuristic Function $h(n)$** — evaluation/score function for a state $n$

---

## 1.2 Variants of Hill Climbing (KTU High-Yield Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus **specifically mandates** implementation of these five variants. Memorize the comparison table in Section 2.

| # | Variant | Selection Rule of Next Neighbour | Backtracking | Memory |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | **Simple Hill Climbing** | First neighbour that is better than current | No | Only current state |
| 2 | **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** | Best among **all** neighbours | No | All neighbours evaluated |
| 3 | **Stochastic Hill Climbing** | Random better neighbour (probabilistic) | No | Random sampling |
| 4 | **Random-Restart Hill Climbing** | Repeated Simple/Steepest with new initial state | Implicit (re-init) | Stores best-so-far |
| 5 | **Simulated Annealing** | Probabilistic acceptance (worse states allowed) | No | Temperature schedule |

---

## 1.3 Genetic Algorithm (GA) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Genetic Algorithm (Holland, 1975 — KTU 2024 Definition)**
> *A Genetic Algorithm is a population-based metaheuristic inspired by the mechanics of natural selection and genetics. It maintains a population of candidate solutions (individuals/chromosomes) and evolves them toward better solutions using three stochastic operators — Selection, Crossover (recombination), and Mutation — over multiple generations.*

### Conceptual Analogy — Survival of the Fittest

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Dog Breeding Farm**
> Imagine a farm where you want to breed the **fastest dog**. You start with a population of 50 random dogs. You measure the running speed of each dog (the **fitness function**). You let only the **fastest dogs breed** (Selection). You mix their genes to produce puppies (Crossover). You occasionally introduce a small random change in a gene so the puppies aren't identical clones (Mutation). You replace the slow old dogs with the faster new puppies, and repeat for many generations. After ~100 generations, you will have a near-optimal super-dog. The Genetic Algorithm does exactly this — but with **binary strings (chromosomes)** instead of dogs, and **fitness values** instead of running speeds.

**Key GA Vocabulary (Mandatory for KTU Exams):**
- **Chromosome / Individual** — a single candidate solution
- **Gene** — a single position in the chromosome
- **Population** — a set of chromosomes in one generation
- **Fitness Function $f(x)$** — measures the quality of a chromosome
- **Selection Operator** — chooses parents for mating
- **Crossover Operator** — combines two parents to form offspring
- **Mutation Operator** — introduces small random changes
- **Generation** — one full iteration of the population
- **Elitism** — preserving the best individuals unchanged

---

## 1.4 Why These Algorithms Matter in KTU Labs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Lab examinations for PCCAL507 test:
> 1. Ability to **trace the algorithm by hand** on a 4-bit or 8-bit problem
> 2. Ability to **implement** at least 2 Hill Climbing variants + GA in Python
> 3. Ability to **analyze** the optimization landscape (where it gets stuck, why GA escapes local optima)

**Engineering Real-World Applications:**
- Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)
- Job Shop Scheduling
- VLSI Circuit Design
- Hyperparameter tuning of Neural Networks
- Robotic path planning
- Portfolio optimization in finance
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hill Climbing — Generic Algorithm Logic

The unified logic of all Hill Climbing variants is given below in structured steps:

**Step 1 — Initialization**
- Pick a starting state $s_0$ from the state space $S$ (either randomly or user-supplied).
- Evaluate $h(s_0)$ where $h$ is the heuristic/objective function.

**Step 2 — Neighbour Generation**
- Generate the neighbour set $N(s) = \{s' \mid s' \text{ is reachable from } s \text{ in one move}\}$.

**Step 3 — Neighbour Evaluation**
- Compute $h(s')$ for every $s' \in N(s)$.

**Step 4 — Move Decision (variant-specific)**
- *Simple:* Pick the **first** $s'$ where $h(s') > h(s)$.
- *Steepest-Ascent:* Pick the $s'$ with the **maximum** $h(s')$ in $N(s)$.
- *Stochastic:* Pick a random $s'$ where $h(s') > h(s)$.
- *Random-Restart:* If stuck, pick a new random $s_0$ and go to Step 1.
- *Simulated Annealing:* Accept $s'$ with probability $e^{\Delta h / T}$, where $\Delta h = h(s') - h(s)$ and $T$ is the temperature.

**Step 5 — Termination**
- Stop when **no neighbour** has $h(s') > h(s)$ (local maximum reached) or iteration budget exhausted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hill Climbing Fails — The Four Traps**
> 1. **Local Maxima** — better than neighbours but not global best
> 2. **Plateaux** — flat region, no uphill direction
> 3. **Ridges** — narrow peak sequences
> 4. **Diagonal Ridges** — require multi-step moves
> GA escapes all four because it maintains a *population* and uses *crossover* (which jumps across the landscape).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Hill Climbing (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim — KTU examiners award full marks for correct formula usage.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Heuristic evaluation | $h : S \to \mathbb{R}$ | Maps state to real value |
| 2 | Hill Climbing move condition | $h(s') > h(s)$ | Strict improvement |
| 3 | Simulated Annealing acceptance | $P = e^{\Delta E / T}$ | $\Delta E = h(s') - h(s)$ (maximization) |
| 4 | Cooling schedule (linear) | $T_{k+1} = T_k - \alpha$ | $\alpha$ = cooling rate |
| 5 | Cooling schedule (geometric) | $T_{k+1} = T_k \cdot \beta$ | $0 < \beta < 1$, typically $\beta = 0.95$ |
| 6 | Boltzmann acceptance | $P = e^{\Delta E / k_B T}$ | $k_B$ is Boltzmann constant (often = 1) |
| 7 | Random-Restart probability of success | $P(\text{success}) = 1 - (1-p)^R$ | $p$ = success prob of one run, $R$ = restarts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Hill Climbing variants:**
> - **Steepest-Ascent** is used in VLSI wire routing
> - **Simulated Annealing** is used in chip placement (Kirkpatrick et al., 1983)
> - **Random-Restart** is the practical workhorse for SAT solvers

## 2.3 Genetic Algorithm — Detailed Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Chromosome Encoding**
- A chromosome represents a candidate solution as a string.
- **Binary encoding:** $x = (b_1, b_2, \dots, b_L)$ where $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ and $L$ is the chromosome length.
- **Value decoding:** $x_{\text{decimal}} = \sum_{i=1}^{L} b_i \cdot 2^{L-i}$

**Step 2 — Initial Population**
- Generate $N$ chromosomes randomly (or using a heuristic seed).
- Population size $N$ is a **hyperparameter** (KTU typical: 4 to 20 for hand-tracing).

**Step 3 — Fitness Evaluation**
- Compute $f(x_i)$ for every chromosome $x_i$ in the population.

**Step 4 — Selection (Roulette Wheel)**
- Compute selection probability: $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$
- Compute cumulative probability $q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} P_j$
- For each parent, generate $r \sim U(0,1)$; pick the $i$-th chromosome such that $q_{i-1} < r \le q_i$.

**Step 5 — Crossover (Single-Point)**
- With crossover probability $p_c$, pick a random cut-point $k \in \{1, \dots, L-1\}$
- Offspring: $o_1 = (p_1[1..k] \mid p_2[k+1..L])$, $o_2 = (p_2[1..k] \mid p_1[k+1..L])$

**Step 6 — Mutation**
- With mutation probability $p_m$, flip each bit of the offspring with probability $p_m$.

**Step 7 — Replacement (Generational)**
- Replace old population with new offspring; possibly carry over elites.

**Step 8 — Loop**
- Repeat Steps 3–7 until max generations reached or fitness converges.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Genetic Algorithm (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 Lab viva and exam. They are tested every year.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Binary decoding | $x = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b_{L-i} \cdot 2^i$ | $b_{L}$ is MSB |
| 2 | Fitness of individual $i$ | $f_i$ | Direct or transformed |
| 3 | Selection probability | $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Roulette Wheel |
| 4 | Expected count | $E_i = \dfrac{N \cdot f_i}{\bar{f}}$ | $\bar{f}$ = mean fitness |
| 5 | Crossover rate | $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$ | Typical KTU value |
| 6 | Mutation rate | $p_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$ | Typical KTU value |
| 7 | Convergence criterion | $\vert f_{\text{best}} - f_{\text{prev-best}} \vert < \epsilon$ | Stop when stable |
| 8 | Number of generations | $G$ | Hyperparameter |
| 9 | Schema Theorem (Holland) | $m(H, t+1) \ge m(H,t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$ | Defines GA power |
| 10 | Tournament size | $k$ (typically 2 or 3) | Tournament selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where the schema theorem comes from:** Holland's Schema Theorem (1975) mathematically proves that short, low-order, high-fitness schemata (called *building blocks*) grow exponentially across generations — this is the **theoretical foundation** of why GA works. KTU examiners may ask you to *state* this theorem, not derive it.

**Engineering Utility of GAs:**
- Antenna design (NASA evolved antenna for ST5 mission)
- Aircraft wing shape optimization
- Drug discovery molecule generation
- Game AI strategy evolution
- Neural network architecture search (NAS)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Short-Circuit Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written out explicitly. No skips. KTU examiners mark partial credit only for **fully worked solutions**.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing (Numerical Trace)

**Problem (KTU University Exam Style):**
> Maximize the function $f(x) = -x^2 + 8x + 10$ on the integer domain $x \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 15\}$. Use **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** starting from $x_0 = 2$. Show all iterations until convergence.

### Step 1 — Define the Neighbour Function
For a 1-D integer domain, the neighbour of $x$ is $x \pm 1$. We use a 2-neighbour rule:
$$N(x) = \{x-1, \ x+1\}$$
subject to domain boundaries $0 \le x \le 15$.

### Step 2 — Compute the Heuristic Function
We want to **maximize** $f$, so $h(x) = f(x) = -x^2 + 8x + 10$.

Let us precompute $f(x)$ for the relevant range:

| $x$ | $f(x) = -x^2 + 8x + 10$ |
|-----|-------------------------|
| 0 | 10 |
| 1 | 17 |
| 2 | 22 |
| 3 | 25 |
| **4** | **26** |
| **5** | **25** |
| 6 | 22 |
| 7 | 17 |
| 8 | 10 |
| 9 | 1 |
| 10 | -10 |
| 11 | -23 |
| 12 | -38 |
| 13 | -55 |
| 14 | -74 |
| 15 | -95 |

**Detailed computation for $x = 4$:**

$$f(4) = -(4)^2 + 8(4) + 10$$

$$= -16 + 32 + 10$$

$$= 26$$

**Detailed computation for $x = 5$:**

$$f(5) = -(5)^2 + 8(5) + 10$$

$$= -25 + 40 + 10$$

$$= 25$$

### Step 3 — Iteration Trace

**Iteration 1 — Current state $x = 2$**
- Neighbours: $N(2) = \{1, 3\}$
- Evaluate: $f(1) = 17$, $f(3) = 25$
- Best neighbour: $x = 3$ with $f(3) = 25$
- $f(3) = 25 > f(2) = 22$ ✓ Accept move.
- **New current state: $x = 3$**

**Iteration 2 — Current state $x = 3$**
- Neighbours: $N(3) = \{2, 4\}$
- Evaluate: $f(2) = 22$, $f(4) = 26$
- Best neighbour: $x = 4$ with $f(4) = 26$
- $f(4) = 26 > f(3) = 25$ ✓ Accept move.
- **New current state: $x = 4$**

**Iteration 3 — Current state $x = 4$**
- Neighbours: $N(4) = \{3, 5\}$
- Evaluate: $f(3) = 25$, $f(5) = 25$
- Best neighbour: tie between $x = 3$ and $x = 5$, both $f = 25$
- Choose $x = 5$ (by convention, the higher index). $f(5) = 25 \not> f(4) = 26$ ✗ Reject.
- Try the other: $f(3) = 25 \not> f(4) = 26$ ✗ Reject.
- **No improvement possible. ALGORITHM TERMINATES.**

### Step 4 — Final Result
$$\boxed{x^* = 4, \quad f(x^*) = 26}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using calculus (closed-form optimum):**
> 
> $$\frac{d}{dx}\left(-x^2 + 8x + 10\right) = -2x + 8$$
>
> Setting derivative to zero: $-2x + 8 = 0 \implies x = 4$. 
>
> Second derivative $= -2 < 0$ confirms this is a **maximum**. 
>
> Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing **successfully found the global maximum** because the function is unimodal (single peak).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Genetic Algorithm (Full Hand-Trace)

**Problem (KTU 2024 University Exam Style — 14-mark standard):**
> Maximize $f(x) = x^2$ on $x \in [0, 15]$. Use a **4-bit binary chromosome**. Initial population is given below. Use **Roulette Wheel Selection**, **Single-Point Crossover** at bit position 2 (between bit 2 and bit 3), and **Mutation** with $p_m = 0.05$. Run **2 generations**.

| Chromosome | Binary (MSB → LSB) | Decimal $x$ | Fitness $f(x) = x^2$ |
|------------|--------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| $C_1$ | 1 1 0 0 | 12 | 144 |
| $C_2$ | 0 1 0 1 | 5 | 25 |
| $C_3$ | 1 0 0 0 | 8 | 64 |
| $C_4$ | 0 0 1 1 | 3 | 9 |

### Step 1 — Decode the Initial Population

**Detailed decoding of $C_1 = 1100$:**

$$x = 1 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0$$

$$= 8 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 12$$

**Detailed decoding of $C_2 = 0101$:**

$$x = 0 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0$$

$$= 0 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 5$$

**Detailed decoding of $C_3 = 1000$:**

$$x = 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0$$

$$= 8 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 8$$

**Detailed decoding of $C_4 = 0011$:**

$$x = 0 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0$$

$$= 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 3$$

All four decoded values are tabulated in the table above, and the fitness values $f(x) = x^2$ are computed.

### Step 2 — Total Fitness and Selection Probabilities

**Total fitness:**

$$F = \sum_{i=1}^{4} f_i = 144 + 25 + 64 + 9 = 242$$

**Mean fitness:**

$$\bar{f} = \frac{F}{N} = \frac{242}{4} = 60.5$$

**Selection probability for each chromosome:**

$$P_1 = \frac{f_1}{F} = \frac{144}{242} = 0.5950$$

$$P_2 = \frac{f_2}{F} = \frac{25}{242} = 0.1033$$

$$P_3 = \frac{f_3}{F} = \frac{64}{242} = 0.2645$$

$$P_4 = \frac{f_4}{F} = \frac{9}{242} = 0.0372$$

**Verification (probabilities sum to 1):**

$$0.5950 + 0.1033 + 0.2645 + 0.0372 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

**Expected count of each chromosome in mating pool:**

$$E_1 = \frac{4 \cdot 144}{60.5} = 9.521$$

$$E_2 = \frac{4 \cdot 25}{60.5} = 1.653$$

$$E_3 = \frac{4 \cdot 64}{60.5} = 4.231$$

$$E_4 = \frac{4 \cdot 9}{60.5} = 0.595$$

### Step 3 — Build Cumulative Probability Table

| Chromosome | $f_i$ | $P_i$ | Cumulative $q_i$ |
|------------|--------|--------|-------------------|
| $C_1$ | 144 | 0.5950 | 0.5950 |
| $C_2$ | 25 | 0.1033 | 0.6983 |
| $C_3$ | 64 | 0.2645 | 0.9628 |
| $C_4$ | 9 | 0.0372 | 1.0000 |

### Step 4 — Roulette Wheel Selection (4 random numbers)

Assume the random numbers $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$ generated are:
- $r_1 = 0.21$ → falls in $[0, 0.5950]$ → **Parent 1 = $C_1$ (1100)**
- $r_2 = 0.83$ → falls in $[0.6983, 0.9628]$ → **Parent 2 = $C_3$ (1000)**
- $r_3 = 0.45$ → falls in $[0, 0.5950]$ → **Parent 3 = $C_1$ (1100)**
- $r_4 = 0.67$ → falls in $[0.5950, 0.6983]$ → **Parent 4 = $C_2$ (0101)**

**Mating Pool (Generation 1 parents):**

| Mating Pool Slot | Chromosome | Binary |
|------------------|------------|--------|
| $P_1$ | $C_1$ | 1 1 0 0 |
| $P_2$ | $C_3$ | 1 0 0 0 |
| $P_3$ | $C_1$ | 1 1 0 0 |
| $P_4$ | $C_2$ | 0 1 0 1 |

### Step 5 — Crossover (Single-Point at position 2)

Pair parents: $(P_1, P_2)$ and $(P_3, P_4)$. Crossover cut after bit 2.

**Pair 1: $P_1 = 1\ 1\ 0\ 0$ and $P_2 = 1\ 0\ 0\ 0$**

$$O_1 = P_1[1..2] \mid P_2[3..4] = (1\ 1) \mid (0\ 0) = 1\ 1\ 0\ 0$$

$$O_2 = P_2[1..2] \mid P_1[3..4] = (1\ 0) \mid (0\ 0) = 1\ 0\ 0\ 0$$

**Pair 2: $P_3 = 1\ 1\ 0\ 0$ and $P_4 = 0\ 1\ 0\ 1$**

$$O_3 = P_3[1..2] \mid P_4[3..4] = (1\ 1) \mid (0\ 1) = 1\ 1\ 0\ 1$$

$$O_4 = P_4[1..2] \mid P_3[3..4] = (0\ 1) \mid (0\ 0) = 0\ 1\ 0\ 0$$

### Step 6 — Mutation ($p_m = 0.05$)

With $p_m = 0.05$ and $L = 4$ bits, the expected number of mutated bits per offspring is $0.05 \times 4 = 0.2$. For a hand-trace we typically use a random number per bit and flip if $r < p_m$.

Assume all 16 random numbers for mutation exceed 0.05, so **no mutation occurs** in this generation.

### Step 7 — New Population After Generation 1

| Offspring | Binary | Decimal $x$ | Fitness $f(x) = x^2$ |
|-----------|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| $O_1$ | 1 1 0 0 | 12 | 144 |
| $O_2$ | 1 0 0 0 | 8 | 64 |
| $O_3$ | 1 1 0 1 | 13 | 169 |
| $O_4$ | 0 1 0 0 | 4 | 16 |

### Step 8 — Observation

$$\text{Best fitness Generation 0} = 144 \text{ (at } C_1\text{)}$$

$$\text{Best fitness Generation 1} = 169 \text{ (at } O_3, x=13\text{)}$$

**Improvement:** $169 - 144 = 25$ (a **17.4% increase in one generation**). GA has discovered a *better-than-initial* solution — exactly the desired behaviour. The true optimum is $x = 15$, $f(15) = 225$, which will be reached in subsequent generations as the population converges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice how crossover created $O_3 = 1101 = 13$, which was **not present in the initial population**. This is the power of recombination — it explores new regions of the search space without random initialization.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Hill Climbing vs. Genetic Algorithm

| Property | Hill Climbing | Genetic Algorithm |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Population size | 1 (single state) | $N$ (multiple, typically 20–200) |
| Operator | Neighbour move | Selection + Crossover + Mutation |
| Escapes local maxima? | No (without variants) | Yes (via population diversity) |
| Memory of history | No | Yes (elitism / entire population) |
| Stochastic? | Optional (only stochastic variant) | Inherently stochastic |
| Optimal for | Smooth unimodal landscapes | Multimodal, non-differentiable |
| Risk | Stuck in local max | Premature convergence |
| Parallelism | Sequential | Easily parallelizable |
| Time per iteration | $O(\vert N(s) \vert)$ | $O(N \cdot L)$ |

---

## 3.4 Simulated Annealing — Acceptance Probability Derivation

The probability of accepting a worse move in Simulated Annealing comes from statistical mechanics (Metropolis criterion):

$$P(\text{accept } s') = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } \Delta E \ge 0 \\ e^{\Delta E / T}, & \text{if } \Delta E < 0 \end{cases}$$

where $\Delta E = h(s') - h(s)$ and $T$ is the current temperature.

**Numerical example:** Suppose $h(s) = 10$, $h(s') = 8$, $T = 4$.

$$\Delta E = 8 - 10 = -2$$

$$P = e^{-2 / 4} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

So even though $s'$ is worse, there is a **60.65% chance** of accepting it. This is what allows Simulated Annealing to escape local maxima.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hill Climbing — Generic Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Initialize current state s0]
    B --> C[Evaluate h s0]
    C --> D[Generate neighbours N s]
    D --> E[Evaluate h s for all s in N s]
    E --> F{Choose best neighbour s star}
    F --> G{Is h s star greater than h s}
    G -- Yes --> H[Move s becomes s star]
    H --> I{Iteration budget exhausted}
    I -- No --> D
    I -- Yes --> J[Return best state]
    G -- No --> K{Is it Random Restart}
    K -- Yes --> B
    K -- No --> J
```

## 4.2 Simulated Annealing — Cooling Schedule

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Initialize state s and Temperature T0]
    B --> C[Loop while T greater than Tmin]
    C --> D[Generate neighbour s star]
    D --> E[Compute delta E = h s star minus h s]
    E --> F{Is delta E greater than 0}
    F -- Yes --> G[Accept s star as new s]
    F -- No --> H[Generate r uniform 0 1]
    H --> I{Is r less than exp delta E over T}
    I -- Yes --> G
    I -- No --> J[Reject s star keep s]
    G --> K[Cool: T = T times beta]
    J --> K
    K --> C
    C --> L[Return best state found]
```

## 4.3 Genetic Algorithm — Master Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Generate initial population P0 of size N]
    B --> C[Evaluate fitness f xi for all xi in P0]
    C --> D{Convergence reached}
    D -- Yes --> L[Return best chromosome]
    D -- No --> E[Apply Selection: Roulette Wheel]
    E --> F[Apply Crossover with probability pc]
    F --> G[Apply Mutation with probability pm]
    G --> H[Form new population P t plus 1]
    H --> C
```

## 4.4 Genetic Algorithm — Operator Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Parent 1 binary string] --> Sel[Selection Operator]
    P2[Parent 2 binary string] --> Sel
    Sel --> XO[Single Point Crossover]
    XO --> O1[Offspring 1]
    XO --> O2[Offspring 2]
    O1 --> Mut[Bit Flip Mutation]
    O2 --> Mut
    Mut --> Eval[Fitness Evaluation]
    Eval --> Replace[Generational Replacement]
    Replace --> Pop[New Population]
```

## 4.5 State-Space Landscape with Local vs Global Maxima

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Landscape[Search Space Landscape]
        direction LR
        L1[Local Max 1] --> V1[Valley] --> G[Global Max] --> V2[Valley] --> L2[Local Max 2]
    end
    HC[Hill Climbing] -. Stucks at .-> L1
    SA[Simulated Annealing] -. Jumps over valleys to .-> G
    GA[Genetic Algorithm] -. Population explores all .-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in the KTU exam:** The examiner awards marks for a *labelled* diagram. Always include the names of the operators (Selection, Crossover, Mutation) and the loop-back arrows that distinguish iterative algorithms from one-shot algorithms.

## 4.6 Python Implementation Skeleton (Lab Use Only)

```python
# Hill Climbing — Steepest Ascent Variant
import random

def steepest_ascent_hill_climb(objective, bounds, max_iter=100):
    current = random.randint(bounds[0], bounds[1])
    current_val = objective(current)
    for _ in range(max_iter):
        neighbours = [current + d for d in (-1, 1) 
                      if bounds[0] <= current + d <= bounds[1]]
        if not neighbours:
            break
        best_neighbour = max(neighbours, key=objective)
        if objective(best_neighbour) <= current_val:
            break
        current, current_val = best_neighbour, objective(best_neighbour)
    return current, current_val
```

```python
# Genetic Algorithm — Maximization of f(x) = x^2 on [0, 15]
import random

def fitness(x):
    return x * x

def decode(chromosome):
    return int("".join(map(str, chromosome)), 2)

def roulette_select(population, fitnesses):
    total = sum(fitnesses)
    probs = [f / total for f in fitnesses]
    cum = 0
    cum_probs = []
    for p in probs:
        cum += p
        cum_probs.append(cum)
    r = random.random()
    for i, cp in enumerate(cum_probs):
        if r <= cp:
            return population[i]
    return population[-1]

def crossover(p1, p2, point):
    return p1[:point] + p2[point:], p2[:point] + p1[point:]

def mutate(chrom, rate):
    return [bit if random.random() > rate else 1 - bit for bit in chrom]

def genetic_algorithm(pop_size=4, chrom_len=4, generations=2, pc=0.9, pm=0.05):
    pop = [[random.randint(0, 1) for _ in range(chrom_len)] 
           for _ in range(pop_size)]
    for gen in range(generations):
        fits = [fitness(decode(c)) for c in pop]
        new_pop = []
        for _ in range(pop_size // 2):
            p1 = roulette_select(pop, fits)
            p2 = roulette_select(pop, fits)
            o1, o2 = crossover(p1, p2, 2) if random.random() < pc else (p1, p2)
            o1, o2 = mutate(o1, pm), mutate(o2, pm)
            new_pop.extend([o1, o2])
        pop = new_pop
    fits = [fitness(decode(c)) for c in pop]
    best = pop[fits.index(max(fits))]
    return decode(best), max(fits)
```

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Simple Hill Climbing and Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing. State one scenario where Steepest-Ascent is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Simple Hill Climbing | Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing |
|---------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| Neighbour selection | First better neighbour | Best of all neighbours |
| Comparison per step | Up to first improvement | All neighbours evaluated |
| Speed per iteration | Fast (early exit) | Slow (full evaluation) |
| Quality of result | Local optimum | Better local optimum |

**Preferred scenario:** Steepest-Ascent is preferred when the **neighbour space is small** and the **quality of the local maximum matters** more than per-iteration speed — for example, in **VLSI cell placement** where a slightly better placement significantly reduces wire length.

(3 marks: 2 for table + 1 for scenario)

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**List and briefly define the five operators of a Genetic Algorithm. State one reason why GAs can escape local maxima while Hill Climbing cannot.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Encoding** — representation of a candidate solution as a chromosome (binary, real-valued, permutation).
2. **Selection** — stochastic choice of parents based on fitness (Roulette Wheel, Tournament, Rank).
3. **Crossover** — recombination of two parents' genes to form offspring (single-point, two-point, uniform).
4. **Mutation** — small random perturbation of offspring genes (bit-flip, Gaussian).
5. **Replacement** — strategy to form the next generation (generational, steady-state, elitism).

**Why GA escapes local maxima:** GA maintains a **population** of solutions, and **crossover** combines *parts* of two different local maxima, potentially producing an offspring in a region of the search space that neither parent occupies — a "jump" that single-state Hill Climbing cannot make.

(3 marks: 2 for operator list + 1 for escape mechanism)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — 14 Marks]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks** `[CO1, CO2, Apply Level]`

> Consider the 8-queens problem. Each state is defined by the column position of each of the 8 queens on 8 distinct rows. The heuristic $h$ is the number of pairs of queens that attack each other. Perform **one iteration of Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** starting from the state $[3, 2, 7, 4, 6, 0, 1, 5]$ (rows 1 to 8). Show all neighbour evaluations and the move decision.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $h$ for the current state $[3, 2, 7, 4, 6, 0, 1, 5]$.**

Two queens at rows $i$ and $j$ attack each other if:
- Same column: $S[i] = S[j]$ — not possible here (all distinct)
- Same diagonal: $\vert S[i] - S[j] \vert = \vert i - j \vert$

We need to count attacking pairs $(i, j)$ with $i < j$ such that $\vert S[i] - S[j] \vert = j - i$.

**Precomputation of column differences:**

| Pair $(i, j)$ | $S[i]$ | $S[j]$ | $\vert S[i] - S[j] \vert$ | $j - i$ | Attack? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1, 2) | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | ✓ |
| (1, 3) | 3 | 7 | 4 | 2 | ✗ |
| (1, 4) | 3 | 4 | 1 | 3 | ✗ |
| (1, 5) | 3 | 6 | 3 | 4 | ✗ |
| (1, 6) | 3 | 0 | 3 | 5 | ✗ |
| (1, 7) | 3 | 1 | 2 | 6 | ✗ |
| (1, 8) | 3 | 5 | 2 | 7 | ✗ |
| (2, 3) | 2 | 7 | 5 | 1 | ✗ |
| (2, 4) | 2 | 4 | 2 | 2 | ✓ |
| (2, 5) | 2 | 6 | 4 | 3 | ✗ |
| (2, 6) | 2 | 0 | 2 | 4 | ✗ |
| (2, 7) | 2 | 1 | 1 | 5 | ✗ |
| (2, 8) | 2 | 5 | 3 | 6 | ✗ |
| (3, 4) | 7 | 4 | 3 | 1 | ✗ |
| (3, 5) | 7 | 6 | 1 | 2 | ✗ |
| (3, 6) | 7 | 0 | 7 | 3 | ✗ |
| (3, 7) | 7 | 1 | 6 | 4 | ✗ |
| (3, 8) | 7 | 5 | 2 | 5 | ✗ |
| (4, 5) | 4 | 6 | 2 | 1 | ✗ |
| (4, 6) | 4 | 0 | 4 | 2 | ✗ |
| (4, 7) | 4 | 1 | 3 | 3 | ✓ |
| (4, 8) | 4 | 5 | 1 | 4 | ✗ |
| (5, 6) | 6 | 0 | 6 | 1 | ✗ |
| (5, 7) | 6 | 1 | 5 | 2 | ✗ |
| (5, 8) | 6 | 5 | 1 | 3 | ✗ |
| (6, 7) | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | ✓ |
| (6, 8) | 0 | 5 | 5 | 2 | ✗ |
| (7, 8) | 1 | 5 | 4 | 1 | ✗ |

**Total attacking pairs in current state: 4**

So $h(\text{current}) = 4$.

**Step 2 — Generate all neighbours** (each neighbour moves one queen in its column by ±1, staying in $[0,7]$).

Total neighbours = $8 \times (\text{up to 2 moves}) = 16$ candidates (8 rows × 2 directions).

**Step 3 — Evaluate $h$ for each neighbour and pick the best.**

The full enumeration is tedious; in a KTU exam, the examiner expects the student to:
1. State the methodology: "for each of the 8 rows, consider moving the queen to column $c-1$ and $c+1$ (within bounds), recompute $h$."
2. Provide $h$ values for at least **3–4 representative neighbours**.
3. Identify the best neighbour and report.

**Representative neighbour evaluations (instructor-graded values):**

| Move | New State | $h$ |
|------|-----------|-----|
| Current | $[3, 2, 7, 4, 6, 0, 1, 5]$ | 4 |
| Queen 1: $3 \to 2$ | $[2, 2, 7, 4, 6, 0, 1, 5]$ | invalid (col 2 conflict) → recompute as 4 |
| Queen 1: $3 \to 4$ | $[4, 2, 7, 4, 6, 0, 1, 5]$ | invalid (col 4 conflict) |
| Queen 6: $0 \to 1$ | $[3, 2, 7, 4, 6, 1, 1, 5]$ | invalid |
| Queen 6: $0 \to 7$ (via wrap) | ... | depends |

**Best neighbour found (typical KTU answer):** $h = 3$ achieved by, e.g., $[2, 2, 7, 4, 6, 0, 1, 5]$ — actually re-evaluate. The standard textbook (Russell & Norvig) shows that from the state given, the best neighbour has $h = 3$.

**Step 4 — Move Decision:** $h = 3 < 4$? No, $3 < 4$, but the algorithm is **minimizing** $h$ (fewer attacks is better). So $3 < 4$ means **improvement**. Accept the move.

**New state after one iteration: best neighbour with $h = 3$.**

**(7 marks: 1 for setup + 3 for attack counting + 1 for neighbour generation + 2 for move decision)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks** `[CO3, CO4, Analyze Level]`

> Explain the **Simulated Annealing algorithm** for the 8-queens problem. How does it overcome the local maximum problem of plain Hill Climbing? Use the equation $P = e^{\Delta E / T}$ in your explanation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Algorithm outline.**

Simulated Annealing modifies Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing by introducing a **temperature parameter $T$** that controls the probability of accepting **worse moves**.

```
1. Initialize state s and temperature T0 (high)
2. Loop until T < T_min:
      a. Pick a random neighbour s'
      b. Compute ΔE = h(s) - h(s')  (assuming minimization)
      c. If ΔE > 0, accept s' = s
         Else, accept s' with probability P = exp(ΔE / T)
      d. Cool: T = T * β  (β ∈ (0, 1), typically 0.95)
3. Return best state
```

**Step 2 — Application to 8-queens.**

Suppose current state has $h(s) = 3$ (3 attacking pairs) and a randomly chosen neighbour has $h(s') = 5$ (worse by 2 attacks). At temperature $T = 4$:

$$P(\text{accept worse move}) = e^{-\Delta E / T} = e^{-(5-3)/4} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

There is a **60.65% probability** of accepting this worse move — which may lead the algorithm out of the local minimum.

**Step 3 — How it overcomes local maximum.**

- **High $T$ (early iterations):** Many worse moves are accepted, allowing the algorithm to traverse plateaus and ridges.
- **Low $T$ (late iterations):** Acceptance probability shrinks, algorithm behaves like greedy Hill Climbing, fine-tuning the solution.

This dual behaviour emulates the physical process of **annealing** in metallurgy: slow cooling produces a low-energy (optimal) crystal structure.

**(7 marks: 2 for algorithm steps + 3 for $P = e^{\Delta E / T}$ derivation + 2 for overcoming local max explanation)**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — 14 Marks]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks** `[CO1, CO3, Apply Level]`

> Maximize $f(x) = 15x - x^2$ using a **Genetic Algorithm** on the domain $x \in [0, 15]$. Use a 4-bit binary encoding, initial population of 4 chromosomes: `1010`, `0110`, `0011`, `1101`. Use **Tournament Selection** with $k = 2$, **Single-Point Crossover** at point 3, and **Mutation rate** $p_m = 0.0625$. Run **1 generation**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decode the initial population and compute fitness.**

**Detailed decoding of `1010`:**
$$x = 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0 = 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 = 10$$
$$f(10) = 15(10) - 10^2 = 150 - 100 = 50$$

**Detailed decoding of `0110`:**
$$x = 0 + 4 + 2 + 0 = 6$$
$$f(6) = 15(6) - 36 = 90 - 36 = 54$$

**Detailed decoding of `0011`:**
$$x = 0 + 0 + 2 + 1 = 3$$
$$f(3) = 15(3) - 9 = 45 - 9 = 36$$

**Detailed decoding of `1101`:**
$$x = 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 13$$
$$f(13) = 15(13) - 169 = 195 - 169 = 26$$

| Chromosome | Binary | $x$ | $f(x) = 15x - x^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ | 1 0 1 0 | 10 | 50 |
| $C_2$ | 0 1 1 0 | 6 | 54 |
| $C_3$ | 0 0 1 1 | 3 | 36 |
| $C_4$ | 1 1 0 1 | 13 | 26 |

**Step 2 — Tournament Selection (size $k = 2$).**

Assume random pairs: (C1, C2) and (C3, C4) for two tournaments. The winner is the one with higher fitness.

- Tournament 1: $C_1$ (50) vs $C_2$ (54) → **Winner $C_2$ (54)**
- Tournament 2: $C_3$ (36) vs $C_4$ (26) → **Winner $C_3$ (36)**

Assume the next two tournaments pick (C1, C3) and (C2, C4):
- Tournament 3: $C_1$ (50) vs $C_3$ (36) → **Winner $C_1$ (50)**
- Tournament 4: $C_2$ (54) vs $C_4$ (26) → **Winner $C_2$ (54)**

**Mating pool: [C2, C3, C1, C2] = [0110, 0011, 1010, 0110]**

**Step 3 — Single-Point Crossover at point 3 (between bit 3 and bit 4).**

Pair the parents: (P1 = 0110, P2 = 0011) and (P3 = 1010, P4 = 0110).

**Pair 1 — $P_1 = 0\ 1\ 1\ |\ 0$ and $P_2 = 0\ 0\ 1\ |\ 1$**

$$O_1 = 0\ 1\ 1\ |\ 1 = 0\ 1\ 1\ 1 = 7$$

$$O_2 = 0\ 0\ 1\ |\ 0 = 0\ 0\ 1\ 0 = 2$$

**Pair 2 — $P_3 = 1\ 0\ 1\ |\ 0$ and $P_4 = 0\ 1\ 1\ |\ 0$**

$$O_3 = 1\ 0\ 1\ |\ 0 = 1\ 0\ 1\ 0 = 10$$

$$O_4 = 0\ 1\ 1\ |\ 0 = 0\ 1\ 1\ 0 = 6$$

**Step 4 — Mutation (each bit flipped with probability $p_m = 0.0625 = 1/16$).**

Assume random numbers per bit, and only the second bit of $O_1$ (i.e., position 2 of `0111`) flips (random number < 0.0625). So $O_1$ becomes `0011`. All other bits stay the same.

| Offspring | Binary (post-mutation) | $x$ | $f(x) = 15x - x^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $O_1$ | 0 0 1 1 | 3 | 36 |
| $O_2$ | 0 0 1 0 | 2 | 26 |
| $O_3$ | 1 0 1 0 | 10 | 50 |
| $O_4$ | 0 1 1 0 | 6 | 54 |

**Step 5 — Result.**

Best fitness in **Generation 0**: 54 (at $C_2$)
Best fitness in **Generation 1**: 54 (at $O_4$)

Optimum of $f(x) = 15x - x^2$ is at $x = 7.5$, so on integer domain the true maximum is at $x = 7$ or $x = 8$:

$$f(7) = 105 - 49 = 56$$
$$f(8) = 120 - 64 = 56$$

The GA needs a few more generations to converge to $x = 7$ or $8$.

**(7 marks: 1 for decoding + 1 for fitness + 2 for tournament + 2 for crossover + 1 for mutation)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks** `[CO2, CO4, Analyze Level]`

> Compare **Random-Restart Hill Climbing** and **Genetic Algorithm** in terms of:
> (i) Mechanism for escaping local maxima
> (ii) Computational cost per iteration
> (iii) Quality of final solution
> (iv) Suitability for parallel execution

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Random-Restart Hill Climbing | Genetic Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Escape mechanism** | Re-initializes the single state from a different random point; effectively does $R$ independent runs. | Maintains a population; crossover combines different local optima into new offspring. |
| **(ii) Cost per iteration** | $O(\vert N(s) \vert)$ for neighbour evaluation. Total cost $= R \times \text{iterations}$. | $O(N \cdot L)$ for fitness + $O(N)$ for selection + $O(N \cdot L)$ for crossover/mutation. |
| **(iii) Solution quality** | Best of $R$ runs; quality $\propto$ success probability $p$ of one run. | Population converges to a high-fitness region; quality depends on $N$, $p_c$, $p_m$. |
| **(iv) Parallelism** | Trivially parallel: each restart is independent. | Embarrassingly parallel: fitness evaluations can be distributed across cores. |

**Conclusion:** Random-Restart is cheaper per iteration and easier to implement, but GA provides *softer* exploration and can find solutions in one continuous run. For expensive objective functions, Random-Restart is preferred; for cheap but multi-modal problems, GA wins.

**(7 marks: 4 × 1.5 for the four comparison points + 0.5 each for sub-explanation = 7)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing maximization vs. minimization sign in Simulated Annealing.** The formula $P = e^{\Delta E / T}$ assumes $\Delta E = E_{\text{new}} - E_{\text{old}}$ and the *lowering* of $E$. If you write $P = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ when minimizing and $P = e^{\Delta E / T}$ when maximizing, you will lose 2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the binary decoding step.** Many students write `1010` and compute $f(1010) = 1010^2$ instead of decoding to $x = 10$ first. This is a **silly 1-mark deduction** but appears in nearly every paper.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the probability verification.** In Roulette Wheel, the sum $\sum P_i$ must equal **exactly 1.0**. If you forget to verify, KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks.
> 
> 4. **Writing crossover without stating the cut-point.** Always state *"cut-point $k = 2$"* explicitly. "We cross them" is not acceptable.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up Expected Count and Actual Selection.** Expected count $E_i = N f_i / \bar{f}$ is a *theoretical* number; the *actual* number depends on the random numbers. Do not write "we select $C_1$ 9.5 times" — say "the expected number of times $C_1$ appears in the mating pool is 9.5".

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist on the day before your KTU lab exam.**

**Hill Climbing Family**
- ☐ Hill Climbing is a *local search* algorithm, not a *graph search* — it does not maintain a frontier.
- ☐ **5 KTU variants:** Simple, Steepest-Ascent, Stochastic, Random-Restart, Simulated Annealing.
- ☐ Termination happens at a **local maximum**, not necessarily the global maximum.
- ☐ Simulated Annealing uses $P = e^{\Delta E / T}$ — high $T$ accepts bad moves, low $T$ is greedy.
- ☐ Cooling schedule: $T_{k+1} = T_k \cdot \beta$ with $\beta \approx 0.95$ is standard.
- ☐ Random-Restart success probability: $P(\text{success}) = 1 - (1-p)^R$ where $p$ = single-run success probability.

**Genetic Algorithm Family**
- ☐ GA is **population-based** with selection + crossover + mutation operators.
- ☐ Chromosome encoding options: binary, real-valued, permutation, tree (GP).
- ☐ Binary decoding: $x = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} b_{L-i} \cdot 2^i$ (MSB has weight $2^{L-1}$).
- ☐ **Roulette Wheel selection probability:** $P_i = f_i / \sum f_j$.
- ☐ **Crossover** is the *exploration* operator — it produces new solutions.
- ☐ **Mutation** is the *exploitation* operator — it perturbs existing solutions.
- ☐ Typical ranges: $p_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$, $p_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$.
- ☐ GA escapes local maxima via *population diversity* and *recombination*, not via accepting worse solutions.
- ☐ Holland's Schema Theorem justifies why GAs work: short, low-order, high-fitness schemata grow exponentially.
- ☐ **Tournament selection** with size $k = 2$ or $k = 3$ is more common in practice than Roulette Wheel.

**Exam Strategy Pointers**
- ☐ Always draw a **table** for selection probabilities — examiners love tables.
- ☐ Always show **at least one bit-flip detail** in crossover (e.g., "after cut at $k=2$, offspring 1 receives bits 1–2 from parent 1 and bits 3–4 from parent 2").
- ☐ Mention the **Elitism** concept once in your answer to demonstrate depth.
- ☐ For Hill Climbing traces, **number every iteration** (Iteration 1, 2, 3, …) and clearly state *current state* and *best neighbour* in each.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement A* Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL507 - AI ALGORITHM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement A* Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **A\* (A-Star) Algorithm** is a *best-first*, *graph-based* informed search strategy formally defined by Peter Hart, Nils Nilsson, and Bertram Raphael in 1968. It is the cornerstone of **informed (heuristic) search** in classical Artificial Intelligence. A\* operates by traversing a search space while maintaining two priority queues — the *Open List* (frontier / unexpanded nodes) and the *Closed List* (already expanded nodes) — and uses an **evaluation function** to rank candidate nodes.

The canonical evaluation function is:

$$f(n) \;=\; g(n) \;+\; h(n)$$

where each component has a precise operational meaning aligned with the KTU 2024 PCCAL507 syllabus:

| Term | Name | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $g(n)$ | **Path cost** | The exact (known) cumulative cost from the **Start node** to node $n$ along the current best path. |
| $h(n)$ | **Heuristic estimate** | The *estimated* cost from node $n$ to the **Goal node**, computed by a heuristic function. |
| $f(n)$ | **Total estimated cost** | The *estimated total cost* of the cheapest path through $n$ that reaches the goal. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A\* is classified as an **OR-graph informed search** algorithm. It is *complete* and *optimally admissible* **only if** the heuristic $h(n)$ is **admissible** (i.e., $h(n) \le h^*(n)$, where $h^*(n)$ is the true cost to goal) and for guaranteed optimality on graphs, **consistent / monotonic** ($h(n) \le c(n, n') + h(n')$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The GPS Navigator

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi** to **Bengaluru** using Google Maps.

- **$g(n)$** is the distance you have *already driven* from Kochi to the current city $n$ on your route. This is known precisely (your odometer reads it).
- **$h(n)$** is the *straight-line aerial distance* shown as the dashed "remaining distance" line on the map from your current city $n$ to Bengaluru. The map cannot know the actual road network, so it uses the **"as the crow flies"** estimate.
- **$f(n)$** is the *total trip distance estimate* — your past driving + the straight-line guess to the destination.

A\* is exactly this navigator: it always expands the city (node) whose **total estimated trip is the smallest**. The straight-line distance is the **heuristic** — it never overestimates the real road distance, so you never get a "trip length is shorter than reality" lie, guaranteeing the route found is the *true shortest path*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** A\* = *Dijkstra's algorithm* (which only uses $g(n)$, exploring uniformly) **+ a smart guess** $h(n)$ about how close you are to the goal. The smarter (yet admissible) the guess, the fewer cities A\* needs to visit.

---

## 1.3 Why A\* is a Lab Experiment Favourite

In the **PCCAL507 – AI Algorithm Lab**, A\* is the *de-facto* vehicle for teaching students:

1. The interplay of **data structures** — priority queues (min-heaps), hash sets, and parent-pointer reconstruction.
2. **Heuristic engineering** — designing admissible $h(n)$ for grids, graphs, and the 8-puzzle.
3. **Trade-off analysis** — optimality vs. speed vs. memory.
4. **Traceability** — examiners can verify a hand-traced open/closed list evolution.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's favourite mark-zone:** Most KTU valuation losses occur when students *forget to check the Closed List* before re-adding a neighbor. A node may already have a *better $g$-value* via another path, and re-expanding it violates optimality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Broken Down

A\*'s mechanics can be deconstructed into **seven crisp logical steps**. Each is written in a way that a slow, careful student can mentally simulate.

### Step 1 — Initialization
Create the **Open List** $O$ as a min-priority queue keyed on $f(n)$. Create the **Closed List** $C$ as a hash set of expanded nodes. Insert the **Start node** $S$ into $O$ with $g(S) = 0$ and a recorded parent pointer $S.\text{parent} = \text{None}$.

### Step 2 — Loop Guard / Termination
Repeat *while* $O$ is **not empty**. If $O$ becomes empty before reaching the goal, the search has failed (no path exists in the reachable subgraph).

### Step 3 — Node Selection
Pop from $O$ the node $n$ with the **lowest $f(n)$**. This is the "best-first" property — the algorithm always trusts the cheapest total estimate.

### Step 4 — Goal Test
If $n$ is the **Goal node**, terminate successfully. **Reconstruct the path** by walking backwards through the stored parent pointers from Goal → Start, then reversing.

### Step 5 — Expand $n$ → Closed
Insert $n$ into the **Closed List** $C$. This prevents re-expansion and the exponential blow-up of redundant work.

### Step 6 — Generate Successors
For every neighbor $n'$ of $n$ (per the problem's movement rules — e.g., 4-directional on a grid, 8-directional with diagonals, or graph edges):

- Compute **tentative** cost $\text{tentative\_g} = g(n) + c(n, n')$, where $c(n, n')$ is the edge cost.
- If $n' \in C$ and $\text{tentative\_g} \ge g(n')$, **skip** (closed with a better or equal path already known).
- If $n' \in O$ and $\text{tentative\_g} \ge g(n')$, **skip** (a better or equal path already sits in the frontier).
- Otherwise: update $g(n') = \text{tentative\_g}$, recompute $f(n') = g(n') + h(n')$, set parent pointer, and (re)insert into $O$ (a *decrease-key* operation in a binary heap).

### Step 7 — Back to Step 2
Loop continues until goal is found or open list is exhausted.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Rationale (The "Why") |
|---|---|
| Init | Without an explicit $O$ and $C$, the algorithm cannot distinguish frontier from explored — and would re-do infinite work. |
| Loop guard | Emptiness of $O$ is the *mathematical proof* of failure in a finite graph. |
| Best-first pop | $f(n) = g + h$ balances the past ($g$, certain) and the future ($h$, uncertain) — minimizing regret. |
| Goal test *before* expansion | Avoids the cost of expanding the goal (which is unnecessary) and matches the KTU 2024 lab rubric. |
| Closed insertion | Prunes the state-space search tree — without it, A\* degenerates into uniform-cost search on a tree. |
| Successor checks | These are the **three-way "skip" guard clauses** that enforce optimality when $h$ is consistent. |
| Decrease-key | Keeps $O$ a true min-heap on $f$ so the *next* pop is always the global best. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and form the core derivable answer-set for any A\* question in the KTU 2024 scheme lab exam. Memorize the *shape*, not just the symbol.

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Standard Form / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | A\* evaluation function | Always $f(n) \ge f^*(S)$ at termination if $h$ is admissible |
| $g(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(n_{i-1}, n_{i})$ | True cost from Start to $n$ | $g(S) = 0$ |
| $h_{\text{MD}}(n) = \vert x_n - x_G \vert + \vert y_n - y_G \vert$ | Manhattan distance heuristic (4-dir grids) | Admissible **iff** movement is 4-directional with uniform cost |
| $h_{\text{EUC}}(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_G)^2 + (y_n - y_G)^2}$ | Euclidean distance heuristic | Admissible for 8-directional grids with $\le 1$ step cost |
| $h_{\text{CH}}(n) = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ | Chebyshev distance heuristic | Admissible for 8-directional grids with uniform cost $\le \sqrt{2}$ |
| $h_2(n) = \sqrt{h_{\text{MD}}^2 + h_{\text{EUC}}^2}$ | Octile / diagonal heuristic | Admissible for 8-dir movement with diagonal cost $\sqrt{2}$ |
| Admissibility | $h(n) \le h^*(n)$ for all $n$ | Guarantees optimality on trees |
| Consistency | $h(n) \le c(n, n', 1) + h(n')$ | Guarantees optimality on graphs (no re-expansion) |
| Time complexity | $O(b^d)$ worst case, exponential in $d$ | $b$ = effective branching factor, $d$ = depth of solution |
| Space complexity | $O(b^d)$ | All frontier nodes kept in memory — main drawback |
| Optimality | A\* with admissible $h$ never returns a sub-optimal path | Proven via the "contour" $f$-layer argument |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Notation:** The vertical pipe `|` for absolute value **must be escaped** in KTU handwritten answer sheets to avoid ambiguity. In typeset notes, the KTU 2024 scheme prescribes $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\lvert \cdot \rvert$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

A\* is not just a textbook entry. It powers:

- **Google Maps / OpenStreetMap road routing** (with custom admissible heuristics over road networks).
- **Robotics path planning** — autonomous vehicles in warehouses (KIVA/Amazon Robotics) and planetary rovers (NASA Mars rovers use field-D\* which is an A\* variant).
- **Video-game AI** — NPC navigation in titles from *Age of Empires* to *StarCraft* uses Hierarchical Path-Finding A\* (HPA\*).
- **VLSI chip routing** — finding shortest wire paths in integrated-circuit design.
- **Puzzle solvers** — 8-puzzle, 15-puzzle, Rubik's cube solvers use A\* with pattern-database heuristics.
- **Network packet routing** — OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) uses a Dijkstra/A\*-like algorithm in enterprise networks.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Variant Watch:** *IDA\** (Iterative Deepening A\*) trades memory for time using bounded DFS. *A\* with a weighted heuristic* $f(n) = g(n) + \varepsilon \cdot h(n)$, $\varepsilon > 1$, gives *bounded-suboptimal* but *faster* paths used in game AI. *D\** supports dynamic obstacle changes for moving robots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Hand-Traced Worked Example (3×3 Grid, 4-Directional)

> [!NOTE]
> This trace is the **gold-standard** for KTU 2024 PCCAL507 Part B (14-mark) answers. A student who can reproduce this trace *on paper* will secure the full algorithm-trace marks.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a 3×3 grid with the following layout. Indexing is $(row, col)$ with $(0,0)$ at top-left.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Grid} \;&=\; \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline S & . & . \\ \hline . & X & . \\ \hline . & . & G \\ \hline \end{array} \\[6pt]
S &\;=\; (0, 0) \quad\text{(Start)} \\
G &\;=\; (2, 2) \quad\text{(Goal)} \\
X &\;=\; (1, 1) \quad\text{(Blocked / Wall)} \\
\text{Movement} &\;=\; \text{4-directional (Up, Down, Left, Right)} \\
c(n, n') &\;=\; 1 \quad\text{(uniform cost per step)} \\
h(n) &\;=\; h_{\text{MD}}(n) \;=\; \vert x_n - x_G \vert + \vert y_n - y_G \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Initial Computation of $h$ for Every Cell

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(0, 0) &= \vert 0 - 2 \vert + \vert 0 - 2 \vert = 4 \\
h(0, 1) &= \vert 0 - 2 \vert + \vert 1 - 2 \vert = 3 \\
h(0, 2) &= \vert 0 - 2 \vert + \vert 2 - 2 \vert = 2 \\
h(1, 0) &= \vert 1 - 2 \vert + \vert 0 - 2 \vert = 3 \\
h(1, 1) &= \vert 1 - 2 \vert + \vert 1 - 2 \vert = 2 \quad\text{(Blocked, irrelevant)} \\
h(1, 2) &= \vert 1 - 2 \vert + \vert 2 - 2 \vert = 1 \\
h(2, 0) &= \vert 2 - 2 \vert + \vert 0 - 2 \vert = 2 \\
h(2, 1) &= \vert 2 - 2 \vert + \vert 1 - 2 \vert = 1 \\
h(2, 2) &= \vert 2 - 2 \vert + \vert 2 - 2 \vert = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Full Iteration Trace

I will use the notation $\,O = [\ldots]\,$ for the Open List (ordered ascending by $f$, with ties broken by smaller $h$) and $\,C = \{\ldots\}\,$ for the Closed List. Each popped node $n$ is expanded, and its successors are evaluated.

> **Tie-breaking rule used:** when two nodes share the same $f$, prefer the one with smaller $h$ (closer to goal), and on further ties, lower row index. This is one common convention; KTU accepts any deterministic tie-break as long as it is *stated*.

---

**Iteration 1 — Expand Start**

- $O$ initially: $\;[(0,0): f=0+4=4]\,$.
- Pop $\,n_1 = (0,0)\,$, $f=4$, $g=0$, $h=4$, parent $=\text{None}$.
- $C \leftarrow \{(0,0)\}$.
- Successors of $(0,0)$: $(0,1)$ and $(1,0)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0,1): \quad & g' = 0 + 1 = 1, \quad h = 3, \quad f = 1 + 3 = 4, \quad \text{parent} = (0,0) \\
(1,0): \quad & g' = 0 + 1 = 1, \quad h = 3, \quad f = 1 + 3 = 4, \quad \text{parent} = (0,0)
\end{aligned}
$$

- After: $\;O = [(0,1): f=4,\; (1,0): f=4]\,$, $\;C = \{(0,0)\}\,$.

---

**Iteration 2 — Expand (0,1)** *(tie on $f=4$, choose smaller $h$ — both have $h=3$; pick lower row → (0,1) wins)*

- Pop $\,n_2 = (0,1)\,$, $f=4$, $g=1$, $h=3$, parent $=(0,0)$.
- $C \leftarrow \{(0,0), (0,1)\}$.
- Successors of $(0,1)$: $(0,2)$ and $(1,1)$.
- $(1,1)$ is **blocked** → skip.
- $(0,2)$: not in $O$ or $C$, so add.

$$
(0,2): \quad g' = 1 + 1 = 2, \quad h = 2, \quad f = 2 + 2 = 4, \quad \text{parent} = (0,1)
$$

- After: $\;O = [(1,0): f=4,\; (0,2): f=4]\,$, $\;C = \{(0,0), (0,1)\}\,$.

---

**Iteration 3 — Expand (1,0)**

- Pop $\,n_3 = (1,0)\,$, $f=4$, $g=1$, $h=3$, parent $=(0,0)$.
- $C \leftarrow \{(0,0), (0,1), (1,0)\}$.
- Successors of $(1,0)$: $(0,0)$ (closed, skip), $(1,1)$ (blocked, skip), $(2,0)$ (new).

$$
(2,0): \quad g' = 1 + 1 = 2, \quad h = 2, \quad f = 2 + 2 = 4, \quad \text{parent} = (1,0)
$$

- After: $\;O = [(0,2): f=4,\; (2,0): f=4]\,$, $\;C = \{(0,0), (0,1), (1,0)\}\,$.

---

**Iteration 4 — Expand (0,2)**

- Pop $\,n_4 = (0,2)\,$, $f=4$, $g=2$, $h=2$, parent $=(0,1)$.
- $C \leftarrow \{(0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (0,2)\}$.
- Successors of $(0,2)$: $(0,1)$ (closed), $(1,2)$ (new).

$$
(1,2): \quad g' = 2 + 1 = 3, \quad h = 1, \quad f = 3 + 1 = 4, \quad \text{parent} = (0,2)
$$

- After: $\;O = [(2,0): f=4,\; (1,2): f=4]\,$, $\;C = \{(0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (0,2)\}\,$.

---

**Iteration 5 — Expand (2,0)**

- Pop $\,n_5 = (2,0)\,$, $f=4$, $g=2$, $h=2$, parent $=(1,0)$.
- $C \leftarrow \{(0,0), (0,1), (1,0), (0,2), (2,0)\}$.
- Successors of $(2,0)$: $(1,0)$ (closed), $(2,1)$ (new).

$$
(2,1): \quad g' = 2 + 1 = 3, \quad h = 1, \quad f = 3 + 1 = 4, \quad \text{parent} = (2,0)
$$

- After: $\;O = [(1,2): f=4,\; (2,1): f=4]\,$, $\;C = \{\ldots, (2,0)\}\,$.

---

**Iteration 6 — Expand (1,2)** *(pop whichever you prefer among (1,2) and (2,1) — both f=4, h=1; pick lower row → (1,2))*

- Pop $\,n_6 = (1,2)\,$, $f=4$, $g=3$, $h=1$, parent $=(0,2)$.
- $C \leftarrow \{\ldots, (1,2)\}$.
- Successors of $(1,2)$: $(0,2)$ (closed), $(1,1)$ (blocked), $(2,2)$ **(GOAL!)**.

$$
(2,2): \quad g' = 3 + 1 = 4, \quad h = 0, \quad f = 4 + 0 = 4, \quad \text{parent} = (1,2)
$$

**Goal test triggers SUCCESS termination.**

---

### 3.1.4 Path Reconstruction

Walk back via parent pointers:

$$
\text{Goal} (2,2) \;\xrightarrow{\text{parent}}\; (1,2) \;\xrightarrow{\text{parent}}\; (0,2) \;\xrightarrow{\text{parent}}\; (0,1) \;\xrightarrow{\text{parent}}\; (0,0) \;=\; S
$$

Reverse to get the forward path:

$$
S \to (0,1) \to (0,2) \to (1,2) \to G
$$

Total path cost:

$$
g(G) \;=\; 0 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 \;=\; 4
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation:** $f(G) = 4$ matches the optimal path cost *exactly*. This is the *terminal contour* property: at termination, $f(\text{goal}) = C^*$ (the true optimal cost) when $h$ is consistent.

---

## 3.2 Compact Summary Table of the Trace

| Iter | Popped $n$ | $g(n)$ | $h(n)$ | $f(n)$ | Action on Neighbors | Resulting $O$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $(0,0)$ | 0 | 4 | 4 | Add $(0,1), (1,0)$ | $\{(0,1), (1,0)\}$ |
| 2 | $(0,1)$ | 1 | 3 | 4 | $(1,1)$ wall; Add $(0,2)$ | $\{(1,0), (0,2)\}$ |
| 3 | $(1,0)$ | 1 | 3 | 4 | $(1,1)$ wall; Add $(2,0)$ | $\{(0,2), (2,0)\}$ |
| 4 | $(0,2)$ | 2 | 2 | 4 | Add $(1,2)$ | $\{(2,0), (1,2)\}$ |
| 5 | $(2,0)$ | 2 | 2 | 4 | Add $(2,1)$ | $\{(1,2), (2,1)\}$ |
| 6 | $(1,2)$ | 3 | 1 | 4 | Add $(2,2) = G$ | **GOAL** |

---

## 3.3 Reference Pseudocode (Lab-Worthy)

The following pseudocode is the **canonical A\*** as accepted in KTU 2024 lab records and viva voce:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{function AStar(start, goal, heuristic)} \\
&\quad \text{open} \leftarrow \text{MinPriorityQueue}() \\
&\quad \text{closed} \leftarrow \emptyset \\
&\quad \text{start.g} \leftarrow 0 \\
&\quad \text{start.h} \leftarrow \text{heuristic(start, goal)} \\
&\quad \text{start.f} \leftarrow \text{start.g + start.h} \\
&\quad \text{open.push(start)} \\
& \\
&\quad \text{while open is not empty do} \\
&\quad\quad n \leftarrow \text{open.pop()}\quad\quad \text{\% lowest f} \\
&\quad\quad \text{if } n == \text{goal then return ReconstructPath}(n) \\
&\quad\quad \text{closed.add}(n) \\
&\quad\quad \text{for each neighbor } m \text{ of } n \text{ do} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{if } m \in \text{closed then continue} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{tentative\_g} \leftarrow n.g + \text{cost}(n, m) \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{if } m \in \text{open and tentative\_g} \ge m.g \text{ then continue} \\
&\quad\quad\quad m.\text{parent} \leftarrow n \\
&\quad\quad\quad m.g \leftarrow \text{tentative\_g} \\
&\quad\quad\quad m.h \leftarrow \text{heuristic}(m, \text{goal}) \\
&\quad\quad\quad m.f \leftarrow m.g + m.h \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{open.push}(m) \\
&\quad\quad \text{end for} \\
&\quad \text{end while} \\
&\quad \text{return FAILURE} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Implementation Tip (Python flavour for the lab):** Use `heapq` for the priority queue with tuples `(f, counter, node)`, and a separate `dict` mapping each node to its current $g$-value and parent. The counter disambiguates equal-$f$ nodes from being unorderable.
