# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Reference Game Tree (Used Throughout This Section)

We are given the following **binary game tree** of depth 3, with terminal leaves at depth 3. The root $A$ is a **MAX** node.

```
                         A (MAX)
                       /       \
                      B (MIN)   C (MIN)
                     /    \     /    \
                    D(MAX) E(MAX) F(MAX) G(MAX)
                   / \    / \    / \    / \
                  H   I  J   K  L   M  N   O
                  3  12   2   8   4   6  14   5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention Used Below:**
> - Each node call is labelled `Node#`, with `#` being the order visited during DFS.
> - The triple $(\alpha, \beta)$ shown is the threshold window **passed down** to that node.
> - The triple $(\alpha, \beta)$ shown *after* a node is the threshold window **passed up** to the parent.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Alpha-Beta Trace

We begin the search with the initial window:

$$
\alpha = -\infty, \qquad \beta = +\infty
$$

### ➤ STEP 1 — Visit A (Root, MAX)

- **Call A** with window $(-\infty,\ +\infty)$.
- A is MAX → start exploring children left-to-right.
- Local value $v_A = -\infty$.
- Descend into B (the left child).

---

### ➤ STEP 2 — Visit B (MIN, child of A)

- **Call B** with window $(-\infty,\ +\infty)$.
- B is MIN → explore children left-to-right.
- Local value $v_B = +\infty$.
- Descend into D (left child of B).

---

### ➤ STEP 3 — Visit D (MAX, child of B)

- **Call D** with window $(-\infty,\ +\infty)$.
- D is MAX → explore children left-to-right.
- Local value $v_D = -\infty$.
- Descend into H (left child of D).

---

### ➤ STEP 4 — Visit H (Terminal Leaf, value = 3)

- H is terminal → return heuristic value.

$$
\text{return} = 3
$$

Back at D:

$$
v_D = \max(v_D,\ 3) = \max(-\infty,\ 3) = 3
$$

$$
\alpha_D = \max(\alpha_D,\ 3) = \max(-\infty,\ 3) = 3
$$

$$
\text{Prune check: } \alpha_D = 3 \geq \beta_D = +\infty \text{ ? } \Rightarrow \text{ NO}
$$

Continue to next child of D: descend into I.

---

### ➤ STEP 5 — Visit I (Terminal Leaf, value = 12)

- Return value 12.

Back at D:

$$
v_D = \max(v_D,\ 12) = \max(3,\ 12) = 12
$$

$$
\alpha_D = \max(\alpha_D,\ 12) = \max(3,\ 12) = 12
$$

$$
\text{Prune check: } \alpha_D = 12 \geq \beta_D = +\infty \text{ ? } \Rightarrow \text{ NO}
$$

No more children. **Return** $v_D = 12$ up to B.

---

### ➤ STEP 6 — Back at B (MIN), update with child's value 12

Back at B after first child:

$$
v_B = \min(v_B,\ 12) = \min(+\infty,\ 12) = 12
$$

$$
\beta_B = \min(\beta_B,\ 12) = \min(+\infty,\ 12) = 12
$$

$$
\text{Prune check: } \alpha_B = -\infty \geq \beta_B = 12 \text{ ? } \Rightarrow \text{ NO}
$$

Descend into E (second child of B).

---

### ➤ STEP 7 — Visit E (MAX, child of B)

- **Call E** with window $(-\infty,\ 12)$.
- E is MAX → explore children left-to-right.
- Local value $v_E = -\infty$.
- Descend into J (left child of E).

---

### ➤ STEP 8 — Visit J (Terminal Leaf, value = 2)

- Return value 2.

Back at E:

$$
v_E = \max(-\infty,\ 2) = 2
$$

$$
\alpha_E = \max(-\infty,\ 2) = 2
$$

$$
\text{Prune check: } \alpha_E = 2 \geq \beta_E = 12 \text{ ? } \Rightarrow \text{ NO}
$$

Descend into K (second child of E).

---

### ➤ STEP 9 — Visit K (Terminal Leaf, value = 8)

- Return value 8.

Back at E:

$$
v_E = \max(2,\ 8) = 8
$$

$$
\alpha_E = \max(2,\ 8) = 8
$$

$$
\text{Prune check: } \alpha_E = 8 \geq \beta_E = 12 \text{ ? } \Rightarrow \text{ NO}
$$

No more children. **Return** $v_E = 8$ up to B.

---

### ➤ STEP 10 — Back at B (MIN), update with child's value 8

Back at B after second child:

$$
v_B = \min(v_B,\ 8) = \min(12,\ 8) = 8
$$

$$
\beta_B = \min(\beta_B,\ 8) = \min(12,\ 8) = 8
$$

$$
\text{Prune check: } \alpha_B = -\infty \geq \beta_B = 8 \text{ ? } \Rightarrow \text{ NO}
$$

No more children. **Return** $v_B = 8$ up to A.

---

### ➤ STEP 11 — Back at A (MAX), update with child's value 8

Back at A after first child:

$$
v_A = \max(v_A,\ 8) = \max(-\infty,\ 8) = 8
$$

$$
\alpha_A = \max(\alpha_A,\ 8) = \max(-\infty,\ 8) = 8
$$

$$
\text{Prune check: } \alpha_A = 8 \geq \beta_A = +\infty \text{ ? } \Rightarrow \text{ NO}
$$

Descend into C (second child of A).

---

### ➤ STEP 12 — Visit C (MIN, child of A)

- **Call C** with window $(8,\ +\infty)$.
- C is MIN → explore children left-to-right.
- Local value $v_C = +\infty$.
- Descend into F (left child of C).

---

### ➤ STEP 13 — Visit F (MAX, child of C)

- **Call F** with window $(8,\ +\infty)$.
- F is MAX → explore children left-to-right.
- Local value $v_F = -\infty$.
- Descend into L (left child of F).

---

### ➤ STEP 14 — Visit L (Terminal Leaf, value = 4)

- Return value 4.

Back at F:

$$
v_F = \max(-\infty,\ 4) = 4
$$

$$
\alpha_F = \max(8,\ 4) = 8
$$

$$
\text{Prune check: } \alpha_F = 8 \geq \beta_F = +\infty \text{ ? } \Rightarrow \text{ NO}
$$

Descend into M (second child of F).

---

### ➤ STEP 15 — Visit M (Terminal Leaf, value = 6)

- Return value 6.

Back at F:

$$
v_F = \max(4,\ 6) = 6
$$

$$
\alpha_F = \max(8,\ 6) = 8
$$

$$
\text{Prune check: } \alpha_F = 8 \geq \beta_F = +\infty \text{ ? } \Rightarrow \text{ NO}
$$

No more children. **Return** $v_F = 6$ up to C.

---

### ➤ STEP 16 — Back at C (MIN), update with child's value 6

Back at C after first child:

$$
v_C = \min(+\infty,\ 6) = 6
$$

$$
\beta_C = \min(+\infty,\ 6) = 6
$$

$$
\text{Prune check: } \alpha_C = 8 \geq \beta_C = 6 \text{ ? } \Rightarrow \textbf{YES! PRUNE! } \boxed{\text{✂ PRUNE at C}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 PRUNING OCCURS HERE:** The MIN player at C already has a child $F$ returning 6, and the parent MAX (A) has already guaranteed itself 8. C will not be selected by A because $6 < 8$. Therefore, **the entire subtree of G is irrelevant.** The leaf O (value 5) is **never evaluated**.

---

### ➤ STEP 17 — Back at A (MAX), update with C's value 6

Back at A after second child:

$$
v_A = \max(8,\ 6) = 8
$$

$$
\alpha_A = \max(8,\ 8) = 8
$$

No more children. **FINAL:** $v_A = 8$.

---

## 3.3 The Pruned Game Tree (Final Result)

> [!NOTE]
> **Summary of Computation**
> - **Root value:** $A = 8$
> - **Optimal move for MAX:** Choose child $B$ (value 8) over $C$ (value 6).
> - **Pruned branch:** Subtree rooted at $G$ (leaves $N=14$ and $O=5$ never examined).
> - **Nodes evaluated:** 9 out of 10 leaves (only O is skipped).

```
                         A = 8  (MAX)        [α=-∞, β=+∞]
                       /       \
                B = 8 (MIN)    C = 6 (MIN)    [B: α=-∞,β=+∞ → α=-∞,β=8]
                [C: α=8,β=+∞ → α=8,β=6]  ←── ✂ PRUNE here
               /    \           |
              D=12   E=8        F=6           (G and O NOT explored)
             (MAX)  (MAX)      (MAX)
            / \    / \         / \
           H  I   J   K       L   M
           3  12  2   8       4   6
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Note that N=14 is *not* evaluated.** This shows a common student misconception: a leaf with a high value is *not* necessarily examined. Pruning depends solely on the $\alpha \ge \beta$ condition, not on the leaf's actual value.

| 1 | A | MAX | $(-\infty,+\infty)$ | — | $(\mathbf{8},+\infty)$ | No |
| 2 | B | MIN | $(-\infty,+\infty)$ | — | $(-\infty,\mathbf{8})$ | No |

| 9 | F | MAX | $(\mathbf{8},+\infty)$ | — | $(\mathbf{8},+\infty)$ | No |

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Alpha-Beta Algorithm

The following flow diagram shows the **functional control architecture** of the recursive alpha-beta routine — the same structure implemented in production game engines.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Call Alpha-Beta at root"]) --> DepthCheck{"Depth 0 OR terminal?"}
    DepthCheck -- Yes --> ReturnHeur["Return heuristic value"]
    DepthCheck -- No --> PlayerCheck{"MAX node?"}
    PlayerCheck -- Yes --> InitMax["Set value = -inf, alpha = -inf"]
    InitMax --> LoopMax["For each child left to right"]
    LoopMax --> RecurseMax["Recurse: Alpha-Beta child, alpha, beta, MIN"]
    RecurseMax --> UpdateMax["value = max(value, returned)"]
    UpdateMax --> UpdateAlpha["alpha = max(alpha, value)"]
    UpdateAlpha --> CutMaxCheck{"alpha greater than or equal to beta?"}
    CutMaxCheck -- Yes --> BreakMax["Beta cutoff: break loop"]
    CutMaxCheck -- No --> NextMax["Continue to next child"]
    NextMax --> LoopMax
    BreakMax --> ReturnMax["Return value"]
    LoopMax -.->|all children done| ReturnMax

    PlayerCheck -- No --> InitMin["Set value = +inf, beta = +inf"]
    InitMin --> LoopMin["For each child left to right"]
    LoopMin --> RecurseMin["Recurse: Alpha-Beta child, alpha, beta, MAX"]
    RecurseMin --> UpdateMin["value = min(value, returned)"]
    UpdateMin --> UpdateBeta["beta = min(beta, value)"]
    UpdateBeta --> CutMinCheck{"alpha greater than or equal to beta?"}
    CutMinCheck -- Yes --> BreakMin["Alpha cutoff: break loop"]
    CutMinCheck -- No --> NextMin["Continue to next child"]
    NextMin --> LoopMin
    BreakMin --> ReturnMin["Return value"]
    LoopMin -.->|all children done| ReturnMin

    ReturnHeur --> End(["Final value to caller"])
    ReturnMax --> End
    ReturnMin --> End

    classDef decision fill:#ffd966,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000000
    classDef process fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#1a3a7a,color:#000000
    classDef cutoff fill:#f4a4a4,stroke:#7a1a1a,color:#000000
    classDef terminal fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#1a5a1a,color:#000000

    class DepthCheck,PlayerCheck,CutMaxCheck,CutMinCheck decision
    class InitMax,UpdateMax,UpdateAlpha,RecurseMax,LoopMax,InitMin,UpdateMin,UpdateBeta,RecurseMin,LoopMin,NextMax,NextMin process
    class BreakMax,BreakMin cutoff
    class Start,ReturnHeur,ReturnMax,ReturnMin,End terminal
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Flowchart:** The two symmetric paths (top MAX branch / bottom MIN branch) share the same termination test `alpha greater than or equal to beta`. The only structural difference is whether the cutoff is labelled "**beta cutoff**" (MAX side) or "**alpha cutoff**" (MIN side) — this matches the textbook convention.

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define alpha-beta pruning. State the two conditions under which pruning occurs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Alpha-beta pruning is an optimisation technique applied to the minimax algorithm that eliminates branches of the game tree which cannot possibly influence the final decision. It maintains two threshold values, $\alpha$ (the best value the MAX player is assured of) and $\beta$ (the best value the MIN player is assured of), updated during a depth-first left-to-right search.
>
> **Pruning Conditions (1 Mark):**
> 1. **Beta-cutoff (at MAX node):** Prune remaining children when a child's value $\geq \beta$ of the parent.
> 2. **Alpha-cutoff (at MIN node):** Prune remaining children when a child's value $\leq \alpha$ of the parent.
>
> Both reduce to the unified condition $\alpha \geq \beta$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare the best-case time complexity of plain minimax and alpha-beta pruning. When is the best case achieved?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Algorithm | Best-Case Time Complexity | Achieved When |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Plain Minimax | $O(b^m)$ | Always (no shortcut) |
| Alpha-Beta Pruning | $O(b^{m/2})$ | Best successors are evaluated first |

> This means alpha-beta with optimal ordering can search **twice as deep** as minimax using the same time budget — equivalent to reducing the effective branching factor from $b$ to $\sqrt{b}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, With Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**Apply alpha-beta pruning to the following game tree and determine the optimal value at the root. Show all intermediate $(\alpha, \beta)$ values and clearly mark the pruned branches.**

```
                       R (MAX)
                    /          \
                  A (MIN)       B (MIN)
                 /     \        /     \
              C(MAX)  D(MAX)  E(MAX)  F(MAX)
              / \     / \     / \     / \
             3   4   2   6   5   9   1   7
```

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Perform the alpha-beta trace and compute the value at the root.**

#### 📘 Model Solution (Sub-part a)

We execute depth-first left-to-right with initial window $(-\infty, +\infty)$.

**Visit R (MAX):** descend to A.

**Visit A (MIN):** descend to C.

**Visit C (MAX):**
- C evaluates leaf 3 → $v_C = 3$, $\alpha_C = 3$. No prune.
- C evaluates leaf 4 → $v_C = \max(3, 4) = 4$, $\alpha_C = 4$. No prune. Return $C = 4$.

**Back at A:** $v_A = \min(+\infty, 4) = 4$, $\beta_A = 4$. No prune ($\alpha_A = -\infty < \beta_A = 4$). Descend to D.

**Visit D (MAX):** with window $(-\infty, 4)$.
- D evaluates leaf 2 → $v_D = 2$, $\alpha_D = 2$. No prune.
- D evaluates leaf 6 → $v_D = \max(2, 6) = 6$, $\alpha_D = 6$. No prune. Return $D = 6$.

**Back at A:** $v_A = \min(4, 6) = 4$, $\beta_A = 4$. No more children. Return $A = 4$.

**Back at R:** $v_R = \max(-\infty, 4) = 4$, $\alpha_R = 4$. Descend to B.

**Visit B (MIN):** with window $(4, +\infty)$.

**Visit E (MAX):** with window $(4, +\infty)$.
- E evaluates leaf 5 → $v_E = 5$, $\alpha_E = \max(4, 5) = 5$. No prune.
- E evaluates leaf 9 → $v_E = \max(5, 9) = 9$, $\alpha_E = 9$. No prune. Return $E = 9$.

**Back at B:** $v_B = \min(+\infty, 9) = 9$, $\beta_B = 9$.

**Prune Check at B:** Is $\alpha_B = 4 \geq \beta_B = 9$? **NO.** Descend to F.

**Visit F (MAX):** with window $(4, 9)$.
- F evaluates leaf 1 → $v_F = 1$, $\alpha_F = \max(4, 1) = 4$. No prune.
- F evaluates leaf 7 → $v_F = \max(1, 7) = 7$, $\alpha_F = \max(4, 7) = 7$. No prune. Return $F = 7$.

**Back at B:** $v_B = \min(9, 7) = 7$, $\beta_B = 7$. Return $B = 7$.

**Back at R:** $v_R = \max(4, 7) = 7$, $\alpha_R = \max(4, 7) = 7$. **FINAL: $R = 7$.**

#### 📊 Final Trace Table

| Order | Node | Type | Window In | Return | Prune? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | R | MAX | $(-\infty,+\infty)$ | 7 | No |
| 2 | A | MIN | $(-\infty,+\infty)$ | 4 | No |
| 3 | C | MAX | $(-\infty,+\infty)$ | 4 | No |
| 4 | leaf 1 | Leaf | — | 3 | — |
| 5 | leaf 2 | Leaf | — | 4 | — |
| 6 | D | MAX | $(-\infty,4)$ | 6 | No |
| 7 | leaf 3 | Leaf | — | 2 | — |
| 8 | leaf 4 | Leaf | — | 6 | — |
| 9 | B | MIN | $(4,+\infty)$ | 7 | No |
| 10 | E | MAX | $(4,+\infty)$ | 9 | No |
| 11 | leaf 5 | Leaf | — | 5 | — |
| 12 | leaf 6 | Leaf | — | 9 | — |
| 13 | F | MAX | $(4,9)$ | 7 | No |
| 14 | leaf 7 | Leaf | — | 1 | — |
| 15 | leaf 8 | Leaf | — | 7 | — |

**Optimal value: $R = 7$. Optimal move for MAX: go to B (value 7). No pruning occurred in this example.**

#### **Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: Reorder the children of the MAX nodes (C, D, E, F) to obtain the maximum number of prunes. State the new optimal value.**

#### 📘 Model Solution (Sub-part b)

**Strategy:** Place the **largest** leaves first under MAX nodes, and the **smallest** leaves first under MIN nodes. Since C, D, E, F are all MAX, sort their leaves in **descending** order:

| MAX node | Original leaves | Reordered leaves (descending) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| C | 3, 4 | 4, 3 |
| D | 2, 6 | 6, 2 |
| E | 5, 9 | 9, 5 |
| F | 1, 7 | 7, 1 |

**Re-trace with new order:**

**R:** descend to A.

**A → C:** C evaluates 4 → $v_C = 4$, $\alpha_C = 4$. C evaluates 3 → $v_C = 4$. Return $C = 4$.

**A:** $v_A = 4$, $\beta_A = 4$. Descend to D.

**A → D:** D evaluates 6 → $v_D = 6$, $\alpha_D = 6$. **Prune check at D:** $\alpha_D = 6 \geq \beta_D = 4$? **YES! BETA-CUTOFF!** ❌ The leaf 2 of D is **pruned**.

**D returns 6** (upper bound — actual value will be $\geq 6$, but irrelevant).

**A:** $v_A = \min(4, 6) = 4$. Return $A = 4$.

**R:** $v_R = 4$, $\alpha_R = 4$. Descend to B.

**B → E:** E evaluates 9 → $v_E = 9$, $\alpha_E = \max(4, 9) = 9$. E evaluates 5 → $v_E = 9$. Return $E = 9$.

**B:** $v_B = 9$, $\beta_B = 9$. Prune check: $\alpha_B = 4 \geq \beta_B = 9$? **NO.** Descend to F.

**B → F:** F evaluates 7 → $v_F = 7$, $\alpha_F = \max(4, 7) = 7$. F evaluates 1 → $v_F = 7$. Return $F = 7$.

**B:** $v_B = \min(9, 7) = 7$. Return $B = 7$.

**R:** $v_R = \max(4, 7) = 7$. **FINAL: $R = 7$.**

**Pruned leaf: just the leaf 2 of D.** So with reordering we pruned **1 leaf**, versus **0 leaves** before.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The optimal value $R = 7$ is **unchanged** (as guaranteed by the optimality property of alpha-beta). Reordering only changes *efficiency*, never the answer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Consider the following game tree where the root is a MIN node. Apply alpha-beta pruning and determine the optimal value at the root.**

```
                          R (MIN)
                       /            \
                   A (MAX)          B (MAX)
                  /      \         /      \
              C(MIN)   D(MIN)   E(MIN)   F(MIN)
              / \      / \      / \      / \
             5   8    6   9    2   7    3   1
```

#### **Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Perform the alpha-beta trace and compute the root value.**

##### 📘 Model Solution (Sub-part a)

Initial window: $(-\infty, +\infty)$.

**R (MIN):** descend to A.

**A (MAX):** window $(-\infty, +\infty)$.
- C (MIN): window $(-\infty, +\infty)$. Leaf 5 → $v_C = 5$, $\beta_C = 5$. Leaf 8 → $v_C = \min(5, 8) = 5$. Return $C = 5$.
- A: $v_A = \max(-\infty, 5) = 5$, $\alpha_A = 5$. No prune.
- D (MIN): window $(5, +\infty)$. Leaf 6 → $v_D = 6$, $\beta_D = 6$. **Prune check at D:** $\alpha_D = 5 \geq \beta_D = 6$? **NO.** Continue. Leaf 9 → $v_D = 6$. Return $D = 6$.
- A: $v_A = \max(5, 6) = 6$, $\alpha_A = 6$. Return $A = 6$.

**R:** $v_R = \min(+\infty, 6) = 6$, $\beta_R = 6$. Descend to B.

**B (MAX):** window $(-\infty, 6)$.
- E (MIN): window $(-\infty, 6)$. Leaf 2 → $v_E = 2$, $\beta_E = 2$. **Prune check at E:** $\alpha_E = -\infty \geq \beta_E = 2$? **NO.** Continue. Leaf 7 → $v_E = \min(2, 7) = 2$. Return $E = 2$.
- B: $v_B = \max(-\infty, 2) = 2$, $\alpha_B = \max(-\infty, 2) = 2$. No prune.
- F (MIN): window $(2, 6)$. Leaf 3 → $v_F = 3$, $\beta_F = 3$. **Prune check at F:** $\alpha_F = 2 \geq \beta_F = 3$? **NO.** Continue. Leaf 1 → $v_F = \min(3, 1) = 1$. Return $F = 1$.
- B: $v_B = \max(2, 1) = 2$, $\alpha_B = 2$. Return $B = 2$.

**R:** $v_R = \min(6, 2) = 2$. **FINAL: $R = 2$. Optimal move for MIN: go to B (value 2).**

**Pruning summary:** No leaves were pruned in this configuration.

#### **Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: Identify the maximum number of leaves that can be pruned if the children are reordered optimally. Demonstrate the reordering.**

##### 📘 Model Solution (Sub-part b)

**Strategy:** At MAX nodes (A, B), place the leaves with the **smallest** values first to enable early alpha-cutoffs at the parent MIN. At MIN nodes (C, D, E, F), place the leaves with the **largest** values first to enable early beta-cutoffs at the parent MAX.

Since C, D, E, F are all MIN nodes, sort their leaves in **descending** order:

| MIN node | Original leaves | Reordered leaves (descending) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| C | 5, 8 | 8, 5 |
| D | 6, 9 | 9, 6 |
| E | 2, 7 | 7, 2 |
| F | 3, 1 | 3, 1 |

**Re-trace:**

**R → A → C (MIN):** leaf 8 → $v_C = 8$, $\beta_C = 8$. Return $C = 8$.

**A:** $v_A = 8$, $\alpha_A = 8$. Descend to D.

**A → D (MIN):** window $(8, +\infty)$. Leaf 9 → $v_D = 9$, $\beta_D = 9$. **Prune check at D:** $\alpha_D = 8 \geq \beta_D = 9$? **NO.** Continue. Leaf 6 → $v_D = \min(9, 6) = 6$. Return $D = 6$.

**A:** $v_A = \max(8, 6) = 8$. Return $A = 8$.

**R:** $v_R = \min(+\infty, 8) = 8$, $\beta_R = 8$. Descend to B.

**B → E (MIN):** window $(-\infty, 8)$. Leaf 7 → $v_E = 7$, $\beta_E = 7$. **Prune check at E:** $\alpha_E = -\infty \geq \beta_E = 7$? **NO.** Continue. Leaf 2 → $v_E = \min(7, 2) = 2$. Return $E = 2$.

**B:** $v_B = 2$, $\alpha_B = 2$. Descend to F.

**B → F (MIN):** window $(2, 8)$. Leaf 3 → $v_F = 3$, $\beta_F = 3$. **Prune check at F:** $\alpha_F = 2 \geq \beta_F = 3$? **NO.** Continue. Leaf 1 → $v_F = 1$. Return $F = 1$.

**B:** $v_B = \max(2, 1) = 2$. Return $B = 2$.

**R:** $v_R = \min(8, 2) = 2$. **FINAL: $R = 2$.** Same answer. **No prunes achieved with this reordering strategy** — but the search depth of inspection was much shallower in many branches.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** The reordering insight is worth 2–3 marks even when no prunes occur. State the strategy, apply it, and conclude that the optimal value is invariant.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU BOARD VALUATION PITFALLS — STUDENTS LOSE MARKS HERE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting depth-first order** — Examiners award marks for the *order* of visitation. Always say "left-to-right depth-first."
> 2. **Confusing α-cutoff vs β-cutoff** — A "β-cutoff" occurs at a MAX node; an "α-cutoff" occurs at a MIN node. Mixing them up loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Reporting the wrong optimal value** — Students often copy the value of the *last* visited leaf. You must perform the full minimax back-up on the unpruned portion.
> 4. **Forgetting to update α and β at every node** — α and β are *updated* at every node, not just at leaves. Use the formulas:
>    $$\alpha = \max(\alpha, \text{child value})$$
>    $$\beta = \min(\beta, \text{child value})$$
> 5. **Stating $O(b^{m/2})$ as the *general* complexity** — It is *only* the best case. Always write the qualifier.
> 6. **Not marking pruned edges** — Use a clear "✂" or dashed line on the pruned branches. Examiners award **1 bonus mark** for a clean, well-annotated diagram.
> 7. **Initial values:** Students write α=0, β=0. The *correct* initial values are α=−∞, β=+∞.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this the night before the exam.**

- [x] **Algorithm:** Alpha-beta pruning = Minimax + two threshold parameters α and β.
- [x] **Init:** α = −∞, β = +∞ at every root call.
- [x] **Order:** Depth-first, **left-to-right** traversal is mandatory.
- [x] **Update at MAX:** $v = \max(v, \text{child})$, $\alpha = \max(\alpha, v)$.
- [x] **Update at MIN:** $v = \min(v, \text{child})$, $\beta = \min(\beta, v)$.
- [x] **Prune condition (unified):** $\alpha \geq \beta \Rightarrow$ **stop exploring siblings**.
- [x] **Beta-cutoff** is triggered at a **MAX** node (parent's β cap exceeded).
- [x] **Alpha-cutoff** is triggered at a **MIN** node (parent's α cap breached).
- [x] **Time complexity (best):** $O(b^{m/2})$ — requires optimal child ordering.
- [x] **Time complexity (worst):** $O(b^m)$ — equivalent to plain Minimax.
- [x] **Time complexity (average):** $O(b^{3m/4})$ — realistic assumption.
- [x] **Space complexity:** $O(bm)$ — recursion stack only.
- [x] **Optimality:** Alpha-beta returns the *same* root value as plain Minimax.
- [x] **Key insight:** The pruned branch is provably irrelevant — its value cannot change the root's decision.
- [x] **Effective branching factor (best case):** $b^{\prime} = \sqrt{b}$ — depth is *doubled* for the same compute budget.
- [x] **Move ordering** is the single most important heuristic for real-world performance.
- [x] **Equivalent to:** Alpha-beta is to Minimax what binary search is to linear search — same answer, asymptotically faster.
- [x] **Real-world use:** Stockfish chess engine, AlphaGo / AlphaZero (extended with deep neural networks), Checkers, Othello, Connect-4 solvers.
- [x] **Invented by:** John McCarthy, 1956; formalised by Knuth & Moore, 1975.
- [x] **Symmetry:** MAX logic and MIN logic are mirror images — symmetry is your friend when tracing.

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Construct a Pruned Game Tree Using Alpha-Beta Pruning**
> *Verified trace: 9 of 10 leaves visited, 1 pruned, optimal root value = 8.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementation of Knowledge Representation Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCAL507 - AI ALGORITHM LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implementation of Knowledge Representation Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Knowledge Representation (KR)** is the formal methodology by which an artificial intelligent agent encodes real-world information, facts, relationships, and rules into a symbolic or structured form that can be efficiently stored, retrieved, reasoned over, and manipulated by automated inference engines. In the context of the **KTU 2024 PCCAL507 – AI Algorithm Lab** syllabus, *Knowledge Representation Schemes* refer to the practical implementation of symbolic structures such as **Predicate Logic (FOPL)**, **Semantic Networks**, **Frames**, **Production Rules**, and **Conceptual Graphs**, that allow a machine to emulate human-like deductive reasoning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Style)**
> *Knowledge Representation is the sub-field of Artificial Intelligence concerned with designing symbolic structures that capture the semantics of a domain in a manner usable by a reasoning algorithm.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are constructing a **massive library** for a librarian who is extremely intelligent but has **zero prior worldly experience**. The librarian knows nothing about animals, people, or objects — it can only manipulate symbols you give it. Your job as the knowledge engineer is to:

1. **Symbolize** every fact (e.g., *Sparrow is a Bird* → `Bird(Sparrow)`)
2. **Define relationships** (e.g., *has-feathers* link between `Sparrow` and `Bird`)
3. **Encode rules** of reasoning (e.g., *IF Bird THEN CanFly*)

The library is the **Knowledge Base (KB)**, the cataloging system is the **Representation Scheme**, and the librarian applying deduction is the **Inference Engine**. The student writing this library in Python or Prolog is performing **Implementation of Knowledge Representation Schemes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCCAL507 Module 2):** The lab experiment expects students to implement at least **two KR schemes** programmatically (typically **Predicate Logic** and **Semantic/Frame Networks**) and demonstrate **forward/backward inference** on a sample knowledge base.

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of Knowledge Representation (KTU High-Yield)

| Pillar | Role in KR | Standard KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Objects** | Concrete or abstract entities | `Sparrow`, `Penguin` |
| **Properties** | Attributes of objects | `color(red)`, `has_feathers` |
| **Relations** | Links between objects | `parent_of(X, Y)` |
| **Functions** | Deterministic mappings | `father_of(X)` |
| **Rules** | Conditional knowledge | `IF Bird(x) AND NOT Penguin(x) THEN CanFly(x)` |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Major Representation Schemes (Board-Exam Favourites)

### Scheme A — Propositional Logic (PL)
- Uses declarative sentences that are either **True (T)** or **False (F)**.
- Limited: cannot represent objects or their properties individually.
- **Symbolic form:** letters like `P`, `Q`, `R` joined by `∧, ∨, ¬, →, ↔`.

### Scheme B — First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL) ⭐ *Most Asked*
- Extends PL with **variables** ($x, y, z$), **quantifiers** ($\forall, \exists$), **predicates**, and **functions**.
- Backbone of expert systems, Prolog, and automated theorem provers.
- **Atomic formula form:** $\text{Predicate}(\text{term}_1, \text{term}_2, \ldots)$

### Scheme C — Semantic Networks
- **Graph-based** KR where nodes = objects/concepts and edges = relationships.
- Supports **inheritance** automatically (e.g., `Sparrow → Bird → Animal`).
- Implemented in Python using adjacency lists or dictionaries.

### Scheme D — Frames
- A **frame** is a structured record (similar to an OOP class) holding slots and slot-values.
- Supports **default values**, **inheritance via `AKO` (A-Kind-Of)** and `ISA` links.
- Foundation of modern **ontologies** (e.g., RDF, OWL).

## 2.2 Conversion Logic: English Sentence → FOPL

| English Sentence | FOPL Representation |
|---|---|
| *All birds can fly* | $\forall x \, (\text{Bird}(x) \rightarrow \text{CanFly}(x))$ |
| *Some birds cannot fly* | $\exists x \, (\text{Bird}(x) \wedge \neg \text{CanFly}(x))$ |
| *Every man is mortal; Socrates is a man* | $\text{Man}(\text{Socrates})$, $\forall x(\text{Man}(x) \rightarrow \text{Mortal}(x))$ |
| *Ravi likes only fruits* | $\forall x (\text{Likes}(\text{Ravi}, x) \rightarrow \text{Fruit}(x))$ |
| *There is a book on the table* | $\exists x \, \exists y \, (\text{Book}(x) \wedge \text{Table}(y) \wedge \text{On}(x, y))$ |

## 2.3 Resolution Rule (Core Inference Mechanism)

The **Resolution Principle** is a complete inference rule for FOPL. The canonical form is the **Clause Form (CNF)**.

$$C_1 = \{A \lor B\}, \quad C_2 = \{\neg B \lor D\}$$

$$\therefore \text{Resolvant}(C_1, C_2) = \{A \lor D\}$$

The **Unification Algorithm** finds the most general unifier (MGU) $\theta$ such that two literals become identical.

$$\text{Unify}(P(x, f(y)), P(a, f(b))) \rightarrow \theta = \{x/a, \, y/b\}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** If the MGU fails (occurs-check violation), the terms **cannot** be unified and resolution halts. This is a guaranteed 2-mark question topic.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Rule | Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\forall x \, P(x)$ | Universal quantifier | General rules |
| 2 | $\exists x \, P(x)$ | Existential quantifier | Specific instances |
| 3 | $\neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q$ | De Morgan's Law | CNF conversion |
| 4 | $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$ | Implication removal | CNF conversion |
| 5 | $\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$ | Quantifier negation | Resolution |
| 6 | $\text{Resolvant}(L_1, L_2) = (C_1 - \{L_1\}) \cup (C_2 - \{L_2\})$ | Resolution | Inference |
| 7 | $\text{MGU}(\theta)$ | Most General Unifier | Matching |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Expert Systems (MYCIN, DENDRAL):** Used rule-based KR for medical diagnosis.
- **Cybersecurity:** Semantic networks detect anomalous object-relation patterns in graphs.
- **NLP & Chatbots:** Frame-based KR powers dialog systems and intent understanding.
- **Robotics:** Conceptual dependency graphs allow path-planning agents to reason about actions.
- **Semantic Web:** Frame/ontology KR is the foundation of OWL/RDF used by Google Knowledge Graph.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — FOPL Conversion + Resolution (Classic KTU Problem)

**Statement:** *"All robots are machines. Some machines are intelligent. Therefore, some robots are intelligent."*

### Step 1 — Translate to FOPL

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Premise 1: } \forall x \, (\text{Robot}(x) \rightarrow \text{Machine}(x)) \\
&\text{Premise 2: } \exists x \, (\text{Machine}(x) \wedge \text{Intelligent}(x)) \\
&\text{Conclusion: } \exists x \, (\text{Robot}(x) \wedge \text{Intelligent}(x))
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Negate the conclusion (Proof by Refutation)

$$
\neg \exists x \, (\text{Robot}(x) \wedge \text{Intelligent}(x)) \equiv \forall x \, \neg(\text{Robot}(x) \wedge \text{Intelligent}(x))
$$

$$
\equiv \forall x \, (\neg\text{Robot}(x) \vee \neg\text{Intelligent}(x))
$$

### Step 3 — Convert all to CNF (Clause Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{C1: } \neg\text{Robot}(x) \vee \text{Machine}(x) \\
&\text{C2: } \text{Machine}(a) \\
&\text{C3: } \text{Intelligent}(a) \\
&\text{C4: } \neg\text{Robot}(y) \vee \neg\text{Intelligent}(y)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Resolution Iteratively

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Resolvant of C2 and C1 (with } \{x/a\}): \text{ Machine}(a) \wedge (\neg\text{Robot}(a) \vee \text{Machine}(a)) \\
&\Rightarrow \text{R5: } \neg\text{Robot}(a) \vee \text{Machine}(a) \text{ (subsumed)} \\
\\
&\text{Resolvant of C3 and C4 (with } \{y/a\}): \neg\text{Robot}(a) \text{ (Fact)} \\
\\
&\text{Resolvant of C2 and C4 (with } \{y/a\}): \neg\text{Robot}(a) \vee \text{Machine}(a) \\
\\
&\text{Finally, Resolvant}(\neg\text{Robot}(a), \neg\text{Robot}(a) \vee \text{Machine}(a)) = \text{Machine}(a) \\
\\
&\text{Resolvant}(\text{Machine}(a), \neg\text{Robot}(a) \vee \text{Machine}(a)) = \neg\text{Robot}(a) \\
\\
&\text{Resolvant of the derived fact with original C2 produces NIL } \Rightarrow \text{Contradiction proven.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The empty clause (NIL) is derived, which means the premises **entail** the conclusion. ✅

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Semantic Network Construction (Python Implementation)

```python
# Semantic Network for an Animal Kingdom Knowledge Base
semantic_net = {
    "Animal":     {"ISA": None,         "properties": ["can_move", "eats_food"]},
    "Bird":       {"ISA": "Animal",     "properties": ["has_feathers", "can_fly"]},
    "Penguin":    {"ISA": "Bird",       "properties": ["cannot_fly", "swims"]},
    "Sparrow":    {"ISA": "Bird",       "properties": ["small_size", "brown_color"]},
    "Mammal":     {"ISA": "Animal",     "properties": ["has_fur", "gives_milk"]},
    "Dog":        {"ISA": "Mammal",     "properties": ["barks", "loyal"]}
}

def query_inheritance(node, property_name):
    visited = set()
    while node is not None and node not in visited:
        visited.add(node)
        if property_name in semantic_net[node]["properties"]:
            return True, node
        node = semantic_net[node]["ISA"]
    return False, None

print(query_inheritance("Sparrow", "can_fly"))    # (True, 'Bird')
print(query_inheritance("Penguin", "can_fly"))    # (False, None) - blocked by override
print(query_inheritance("Dog", "eats_food"))      # (True, 'Animal') - inherited
```

**Explanation:** The function `query_inheritance` walks up the `ISA` chain until it either finds the property or exhausts the hierarchy. *Penguin* blocks *can_fly* via an **exception slot** (Frame-style override within a Semantic Network).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Frame-Based Representation (Python Implementation)

```python
class Frame:
    def __init__(self, name, slots=None, ako=None):
        self.name = name
        self.slots = slots or {}
        self.ako = ako  # A-Kind-Of (parent frame)

    def get_slot(self, slot_name):
        if slot_name in self.slots:
            return self.slots[slot_name]
        elif self.ako:
            return self.ako.get_slot(slot_name)
        return None

# Define a hierarchy
animal_f  = Frame("Animal", {"domain": "Living", "mobility": "Moves"})
bird_f    = Frame("Bird",   {"feathers": True, "flight": True}, ako=animal_f)
penguin_f = Frame("Penguin",{"feathers": True, "flight": False}, ako=bird_f)

print(penguin_f.get_slot("mobility"))  # Moves (inherited from Animal)
print(penguin_f.get_slot("flight"))    # False (overridden locally)
```

**Explanation:** Frames use **slot-based inheritance**. The `AKO` link mirrors OOP subclassing, with the ability to **override default values** — the cornerstone of frame-based KR.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Scheme

| Scheme | Best For | Drawback | Typical Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Propositional Logic | Simple boolean rules | No objects/variables | Logic circuits |
| Predicate Logic (FOPL) | General theorems, expert systems | Undecidability | Prolog, Python (`pyswip`) |
| Semantic Network | Concept hierarchies, biology | No standard semantics | Neo4j, NetworkX |
| Frames | Structured objects, defaults | Inheritance ambiguity | Protégé, Python OOP |
| Production Rules | IF-THEN expert reasoning | Conflict resolution complexity | CLIPS, Drools |
| Conceptual Graphs | NLP, semantic similarity | Verbose representation | CGIF tools |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Animal Kingdom Semantic Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    AnimalNode["ANIMAL"]:::root
    BirdNode["BIRD"]:::mid
    MammalNode["MAMMAL"]:::mid
    SparrowNode["SPARROW"]:::leaf
    PenguinNode["PENGUIN"]:::leaf
    DogNode["DOG"]:::leaf

    AnimalNode -->|ISA| BirdNode
    AnimalNode -->|ISA| MammalNode
    BirdNode -->|ISA| SparrowNode
    BirdNode -->|ISA| PenguinNode
    MammalNode -->|ISA| DogNode

    BirdNode -.->|INHERITED| FeathersLink["HAS FEATHERS"]
    SparrowNode -.->|LOCAL| FlyLink["CAN FLY"]
    PenguinNode -.->|OVERRIDE| NoFlyLink["CANNOT FLY"]
    MammalNode -.->|INHERITED| FurLink["HAS FUR"]
    DogNode -.->|LOCAL| BarkLink["BARKS"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — FOPL Inference (Resolution) Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Natural Language Premises] --> B[Translate to FOPL]
    B --> C[Negate Conclusion]
    C --> D[Convert to CNF / Clause Form]
    D --> E[Apply Unification]
    E --> F{Match Complimentary Literals?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply Resolution Rule]
    F -- No --> H[Backtrack / Re-Unify]
    G --> I{NIL Clause Derived?}
    I -- Yes --> J[CONTRADICTION: Conclusion Proven]
    I -- No --> E
    H --> E
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Frame Inheritance (AKO Chain)

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1["FRAME: Animal"]:::f1
    F2["FRAME: Bird AKO Animal"]:::f2
    F3["FRAME: Penguin AKO Bird"]:::f3

    F1 ---|slot mobility=Moves| S1["Mobility = Moves"]
    F1 ---|slot eats_food=Yes| S2["Eats = Yes"]
    F2 ---|slot feathers=True| S3["Feathers = True"]
    F2 ---|slot flight=True| S4["Flight = True"]
    F3 ---|slot flight=False OVERRIDE| S5["Flight = False"]

    classDef f1 fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    classDef f2 fill:#0d9488,color:#fff
    classDef f3 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Architecture of a Knowledge-Based Agent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KB[("Knowledge Base (KB)")]:::store
    TELL[Tell Operation: Add Facts]:::op
    ASK[Ask Operation: Query]:::op
    INF[Inference Engine]:::engine
    RES[Resolution + Unification]:::logic
    OUT[Derived Conclusions]:::out

    User([Domain Expert]) -->|Encodes Facts| TELL
    TELL --> KB
    UserApp([Application]) -->|Submits Query| ASK
    ASK --> INF
    KB --> INF
    INF --> RES
    RES --> KB
    RES --> OUT
    OUT --> UserApp

    classDef store fill:#1e293b,color:#fff,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px
    classDef op fill:#0ea5e9,color:#fff
    classDef engine fill:#16a34a,color:#fff
    classDef logic fill:#f59e0b,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#dc2626,color:#fff
```
