# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define Knowledge Representation. List any **four** commonly used knowledge representation schemes.

**Model Answer:**
Knowledge Representation (KR) is the sub-area of Artificial Intelligence dedicated to formally encoding real-world information about objects, their properties, and their inter-relationships into symbolic structures that an intelligent agent can manipulate through automated reasoning.
The four major KR schemes are:
1. **Propositional Logic (PL)**
2. **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)**
3. **Semantic Networks**
4. **Frames**
*(Other valid options: Production Rules, Conceptual Graphs, Scripts, Ontologies.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between **Semantic Networks** and **Frames** as knowledge representation schemes. Mention one advantage of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Semantic Network | Frame |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Graph (nodes + labelled edges) | Record with slots and slot-values |
| Inheritance | Via `ISA` edges traversed manually | Automatic via `AKO` link (subclassing) |
| Default Values | Not inherently supported | Yes, via default slot fillers |
| Best for | Concept hierarchies, lightweight KR | Structured object hierarchies, expert systems |

**Advantage of Semantic Network:** Intuitive, human-readable visualization of relationships.
**Advantage of Frame:** Supports default reasoning and exception overrides, ideal for taxonomies.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

#### (a) Translate the following statements into First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL): **(7 Marks)**
1. *All students in the AI class are smart.*
2. *Some students study machine learning.*
3. *Every teacher likes a student who studies machine learning.*
4. *No student who studies machine learning fails the exam.*

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1)\ \forall x \, (\text{StudentInAIClass}(x) \rightarrow \text{Smart}(x)) \\
&(2)\ \exists x \, (\text{Student}(x) \wedge \text{Studies}(x, \text{MachineLearning})) \\
&(3)\ \forall t \, \exists s \, (\text{Teacher}(t) \rightarrow \text{Likes}(t, s) \wedge \text{Student}(s) \wedge \text{Studies}(s, \text{ML})) \\
&(4)\ \neg \exists x \, (\text{Student}(x) \wedge \text{Studies}(x, \text{ML}) \wedge \text{Fails}(x, \text{Exam})) \\
&\quad \equiv \forall x \, ((\text{Student}(x) \wedge \text{Studies}(x, \text{ML})) \rightarrow \neg \text{Fails}(x, \text{Exam}))
\end{aligned}
$$

#### (b) Construct a Semantic Network for the following facts and answer the query: *"Does a sparrow inherit the property `can_fly` from its parent class?"* **(7 Marks)**

**Facts:**
- *Animal* is the root class — it has property `moves`.
- *Bird* is a kind of *Animal* — it has property `has_feathers`.
- *Sparrow* is a kind of *Bird* — it has property `small_size`.
- *Penguin* is a kind of *Bird* — it overrides `can_fly` to `False`.
- *Bird* has default property `can_fly = True`.

**Model Solution — Semantic Network Structure:**

| Node | ISA | Local Properties | Inherited Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| Animal | — | moves | — |
| Bird | Animal | has_feathers, can_fly=True | moves |
| Sparrow | Bird | small_size | has_feathers, can_fly, moves |
| Penguin | Bird | can_fly=False (override) | has_feathers, moves |

**Query Resolution for Sparrow inheriting `can_fly`:**
1. Start at node `Sparrow`.
2. Check local slots → `small_size` only. `can_fly` not found.
3. Move to parent `Bird` via `ISA` link.
4. Check slots of `Bird` → `can_fly = True` ✅ found.
5. **Answer: Yes**, `Sparrow` inherits `can_fly = True` from `Bird`.

This demonstrates **property inheritance via `ISA` edges**, the core strength of semantic networks.

---

### Question B — (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

#### (a) Explain the **Resolution Principle** in FOPL. Use a suitable example to demonstrate how two clauses are resolved to derive the empty clause (NIL). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Resolution Principle**, formulated by J. Alan Robinson (1965), is a single, complete inference rule for FOPL. Two clauses containing *complementary literals* (e.g., $P$ and $\neg P$) can be resolved by eliminating those literals and combining the rest via the **Most General Unifier (MGU)**.

**Formal Definition:**

$$
\frac{C_1 : L_1 \lor C_1' \quad\quad C_2 : \neg L_2 \lor C_2'}{\text{Resolvant}(C_1, C_2)\theta = (C_1' \lor C_2')\theta}
$$

where $\theta$ is the MGU such that $L_1\theta = L_2\theta$.

**Worked Example — Proving "Socrates is Mortal":**

Premises in CNF:
- C1: $\neg \text{Man}(x) \vee \text{Mortal}(x)$
- C2: $\text{Man}(\text{Socrates})$

Negated conclusion: $\neg \text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$

Resolution Steps:
1. Apply Resolution to C1 and C2 with $\theta = \{x / \text{Socrates}\}$:
2. Resolvant: $\text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$
3. Apply Resolution to this resolvant and $\neg \text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$:
4. Resolvant: **NIL (empty clause)** ✅

Derivation of the empty clause proves the **negated conclusion is false**, hence the original conclusion is logically entailed.

#### (b) Write a short Python program to implement a **Frame-Based Knowledge Base** for the Animal Kingdom with at least three frames, and demonstrate **slot inheritance with override**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Python Program:**

```python
class Frame:
    def __init__(self, name, ako=None):
        self.name = name
        self.ako = ako          # parent frame
        self.slots = {}         # local slot -> value

    def add_slot(self, slot_name, value):
        self.slots[slot_name] = value

    def get_slot(self, slot_name):
        if slot_name in self.slots:
            return self.slots[slot_name]
        if self.ako is not None:
            return self.ako.get_slot(slot_name)
        return None

# Build the frame hierarchy
animal = Frame("Animal")
animal.add_slot("mobility", "Moves")
animal.add_slot("eats",     "Yes")

bird = Frame("Bird", ako=animal)
bird.add_slot("feathers", True)
bird.add_slot("can_fly",  True)        # default for all birds

sparrow = Frame("Sparrow", ako=bird)
sparrow.add_slot("size", "Small")

penguin = Frame("Penguin", ako=bird)
penguin.add_slot("can_fly", False)     # OVERRIDE of parent's default
penguin.add_slot("swims",  True)

# Demonstrate inheritance + override
print("Sparrow mobility :", sparrow.get_slot("mobility"))  # Inherited
print("Sparrow can_fly  :", sparrow.get_slot("can_fly"))   # Inherited (True)
print("Sparrow feathers :", sparrow.get_slot("feathers"))   # Inherited
print("Penguin can_fly  :", penguin.get_slot("can_fly"))   # Overridden (False)
print("Penguin eats     :", penguin.get_slot("eats"))      # Inherited from Animal
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Sparrow mobility : Moves
Sparrow can_fly  : True
Sparrow feathers : True
Penguin can_fly  : False
Penguin eats     : Yes
```

**Explanation:** The `get_slot()` method implements **recursive inheritance lookup** along the `AKO` chain. `Sparrow` inherits all properties automatically, while `Penguin` **overrides** the `can_fly` slot to `False`, demonstrating **exception handling** in frame-based KR.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCCAL507 Lab Viva & ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting to negate the conclusion** before applying resolution — loses 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Skipping CNF conversion steps** — Skolemization, dropping universal quantifiers, and standardizing variables are *mandatory* intermediate steps examiners look for.
> 3. **Confusing `ISA` (instance) with `AKO` (subclass)** in frames/semantic nets — a classic 1-mark trap.
> 4. **Not specifying the MGU** $\theta$ explicitly during unification — write it as $\{x/a\}$ in the answer.
> 5. **Omitting override rules** in frame-based implementations — failing to demonstrate exception handling is a guaranteed 1-mark loss in the program-output section.
> 6. **Using absolute-value pipes `|x|` in answer tables** — always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid LaTeX parsing failures.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Knowledge Representation** = encoding real-world knowledge in machine-processable symbolic form.
- ✅ **FOPL** is the most powerful general-purpose KR; **Resolution + Unification** form its inference engine.
- ✅ **Semantic Networks** excel at concept hierarchies via `ISA` links; **inheritance** is manual.
- ✅ **Frames** support **slot-based inheritance** with **default values** and **exception overrides** via `AKO` links.
- ✅ **Production Rules** (`IF-THEN`) power expert systems; require **conflict resolution** strategies.
- ✅ Always convert FOPL statements to **CNF** before applying **Resolution**.
- ✅ **NIL clause** (empty resolvant) = proof of contradiction = goal proven.
- ✅ **MGU** (Most General Unifier) is the *most general* substitution that makes two literals identical.
- ✅ KTU practical exam expects **two KR schemes implemented** with a working **inference demonstration**.
- ✅ Use **CNF steps, MGU notation, and clear Mermaid-style diagrams** for full marks in the ESE.
- ✅ Practice **negation, Skolemization, and clause form conversion** — they appear in nearly every paper.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement Local Search Algorithms for CSP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAL507 - AI ALGORITHMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implement Local Search Algorithms for CSP # 📘 Module Experiment 2: Local Search Algorithms for CSP ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition — Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP) A **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** is a formal mathematical framework used in Artificial Intelligence to represent problems whose state is defined by a set of variables, each with a domain of allowable values, and a set of constraints that restrict the combinations of values the variables can simultaneously take. Formally, a CSP is a triple: $$\text{CSP} = \langle X, D, C \rangle$$ Where: - $X = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ is the **finite set of variables**. - $D = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$ is the **set of domains**, where $D_i$ contains the allowed values for variable $X_i$. - $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ is the **set of constraints** that specify allowable combinations of values for subsets of variables. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PCCAL507 Module 2:** > Local Search algorithms work with **complete-state formulations** where every variable is always assigned a value, and the search refines a *single current state* by moving to neighboring states. They are the preferred approach for *large-scale, real-world CSPs* such as scheduling, timetabling, VLSI layout, and map coloring. ### 1.2 Definition — Local Search Algorithm A **Local Search Algorithm** for CSP is an iterative procedure that starts from an initial complete assignment (which may violate constraints) and progressively moves to neighbouring states by modifying one or more variable assignments. The aim is to minimize the number of constraint violations or maximize a global objective until an optimal or near-optimal solution is reached. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🎯 > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Mountain Trekker** > > Imagine you are blindfolded on a hilly landscape, and your goal is to reach the **lowest valley** (the global optimum). You cannot see the entire terrain; you can only feel the slope directly under your feet. > > - **Hill Climbing (with random restarts):** You always step downhill. If the ground is flat or slopes upward, you randomly teleport to a new hill. Eventually, you hope to land in a valley. > - **Simulated Annealing:** You mostly step downhill, but occasionally — depending on how "hot" (probabilistic) you are — you take an uphill step to escape shallow dips. > - **Genetic Algorithms:** You maintain a *population* of trekkers. The best trekkers "reproduce" (crossover), occasionally mutate, and the worst are discarded. Over generations, the population converges toward the valley. For a **Constraint Satisfaction Problem** like the classic **8-Queens problem** (place 8 queens on a chessboard so no two queens attack each other): - A state = placement of all 8 queens (one per column). - A neighbour = moving *one* queen within its column to a different row. - The cost = number of pairs of queens attacking each other (ideally **0**). Local search is *extremely effective* here because the search space is $8^8 = 16{,}777{,}216$ states, but only $1.1\%$ of random placements have zero conflicts — and local search reaches one of these in roughly **22 steps** on average. ### 1.4 Why Local Search for CSP? — The KTU Justification | Property | Systematic Search (BFS/DFS) | Local Search | |---|---|---| | Memory Usage | **Exponential** (stores all nodes) | **Constant** (only current state) | | Suitable for | Small, structured problems | Large, unstructured problems | | Optimality Guarantee | Yes (with proper algorithm) | No (but finds good solutions fast) | | Time per Move | Low | Low | > [!IMPORTANT] > For CSPs with millions of variables (e.g., airline scheduling), systematic backtracking is **practically infeasible**. Local search is the de facto industry-standard approach. ### 1.5 Key Terminology — Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU Exams - **Complete State:** A state where all variables have assigned values (constraints may or may not be satisfied). - **Neighbour:** A state reached by a small, locally-defined modification of the current state. - **Objective Function / Cost Function ($h$):** A numeric measure of state quality, usually the number of unsatisfied constraints. - **Global Optimum:** The best possible state in the entire search space. - **Local Optimum / Local Minimum:** A state better than all its neighbours but not the global best. - **Plateau:** A region where neighbouring states have equal cost (no improving move). - **Ridge:** A sequence of local maxima that is hard for greedy algorithms to navigate. --- # 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Architecture of Local Search — Universal Pseudocode Skeleton Every local search algorithm for CSP follows this generalized iterative pattern: **Algorithm: Generic_Local_Search_for_CSP** ``` 1. current ← Generate_Initial_State() 2. for t = 1 to MAX_ITERATIONS do 3. if Is_Solution(current) then return current 4. neighbour ← Select_Neighbour(current, strategy) 5. current ← Decide_Move(current, neighbour, strategy) 6. end for 7. return current // best-so-far solution ``` The only differences between algorithms lie in **Step 4** (neighbour selection) and **Step 5** (acceptance rule). --- ## 2.2 Algorithm 1: Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing (Gradient Descent for CSP) ### 2.2.1 Operational Logic 1. Start with a randomly generated complete state. 2. Evaluate the cost (number of constraint violations) of the current state and all its neighbours. 3. Move to the neighbour with the **lowest cost**. 4. If multiple neighbours tie, pick one at random. 5. Stop when no neighbour has a lower cost (local minimum) or after a fixed number of iterations. ### 2.2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' - **Why?** Greedy descent is computationally trivial: one evaluation pass over neighbours suffices. - **How to escape local minima?** Two common techniques used in KTU exams: - **Random Restarts:** Run hill climbing $N$ times from different random starts and keep the best. - **Sideways Moves (Plateau Walk):** Allow moves to states of equal cost, with a cap (e.g., max 100 sideways moves). ### 2.2.3 Pseudocode for 8-Queens (Cost = attacking pairs) ``` function HILL_CLIMBING(problem) returns a state current ← MAKE_NODE(problem.INITIAL_STATE) repeat neighbour ← a highest-valued successor of current if neighbour.VALUE ≤ current.VALUE then return current current ← neighbour ``` ### 2.2.4 Mathematical Definition $$\text{NextState} = \arg\min_{s' \in \text{Neighbours}(s)} \big[ h(s') \big]$$ where $h(s)$ is the heuristic cost (conflict count) of state $s$. --- ## 2.3 Algorithm 2: Simulated Annealing (SA) ### 2.3.1 Operational Logic 1. Start at an initial state $s$ and a high "temperature" $T$. 2. Pick a random neighbour $s'$. 3. Compute $\Delta E = h(s') - h(s)$ (change in cost). 4. **Acceptance rule:** - If $\Delta E < 0$ (improvement): always move to $s'$. - If $\Delta E \geq 0$ (worsening): move to $s'$ with probability $e^{-\Delta E / T}$. 5. Reduce $T$ according to a **cooling schedule**. 6. Stop when $T \approx 0$ or a solution is found. ### 2.3.2 The 'Why' and 'How' - **Why does probabilistic worsening help?** It allows the algorithm to escape local minima in early phases when $T$ is high. - **How does cooling work?** A standard geometric schedule: $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$, where $0 < \alpha < 1$ (commonly $\alpha = 0.95$). - **KTU Insight:** If $T$ decreases slowly enough, SA is **provably complete** and will find the global optimum with probability approaching 1. ### 2.3.3 The Boltzmann Acceptance Probability $$P(\text{accept worsening move}) = e^{-\Delta E / T}$$ ### 2.3.4 Pseudocode ``` function SIMULATED_ANNEALING(problem, schedule) returns a state current ← MAKE_NODE(problem.INITIAL_STATE) for t = 1 to ∞ do T ← schedule(t) if T = 0 then return current next ← a randomly selected successor of current ΔE ← next.VALUE - current.VALUE if ΔE < 0 then current ← next else current ← next only with probability e^(−ΔE/T) ``` --- ## 2.4 Algorithm 3: Local Beam Search (K-Beam Variant) ### 2.4.1 Operational Logic 1. Generate $k$ random initial states. 2. At each step, generate **all successors of all $k$ states**. 3. Select the $k$ **best successors** (lowest cost). 4. If any state is a goal, return it. ### 2.4.2 The 'Why' and 'How' - **Why?** Useful information is shared among the $k$ parallel searches. - **Stochastic Beam Search variant:** Choose successors with probability proportional to their quality — prevents all $k$ states from collapsing into the same local optimum. --- ## 2.5 Algorithm 4: Genetic Algorithms (GA) for CSP ### 2.5.1 Operational Logic 1. **Initialize** a population of $N$ random individuals (each = a complete CSP assignment). 2. **Evaluate** fitness (lower cost = higher fitness). 3. **Selection:** Pick parents using e.g. tournament selection or fitness-proportionate (roulette-wheel). 4. **Crossover:** Combine two parents' variable assignments to form offspring. 5. **Mutation:** With low probability, randomly change one variable's value. 6. **Replace:** Form new generation; repeat until goal found or generations exhausted. ### 2.5.2 Crossover and Mutation — The Genetic Operators For CSP with binary strings: - **Single-point crossover:** Pick a random index $k$; offspring gets bits $1..k$ from parent A and $k+1..n$ from parent B. - **Uniform crossover:** Each bit independently chosen from one parent with probability 0.5. - **Bit-flip mutation:** Each bit flipped with probability $p_m \approx 1/n$. --- ## 2.6 Algorithm 5: Min-Conflicts Heuristic (Specialized for CSP) ### 2.6.1 Operational Logic (Highly Effective for 8-Queens & Scheduling) 1. Start with a random complete assignment. 2. Repeat: - If no conflicts, return the current state. - Pick a random **conflicted variable** $X_i$ (one involved in a violated constraint). - Set $X_i$ to the value that **minimizes the number of conflicts** (random tie-break). 3. Stop after $MAX\_STEPS$ iterations. ### 2.6.2 The 'Why' and 'How' - **Why is it so effective?** It only considers moves that *directly address* a violated constraint, ignoring unrelated variables. - **Performance:** Solves $10^6$-variable 8-Queens-like problems in linear time on average. --- ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is **high-yield** for KTU University Exam Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorize these before the exam. | # | Algorithm | Neighbour Selection Rule | Acceptance Rule | Key Parameter | Worst-Case Cost | Completeness | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** | Best neighbour (lowest $h$) | Only if $h(s') < h(s)$ | None | $O(b \cdot d)$ per step | No (local optima) | | 2 | **First-Choice Hill Climbing** | First random improvement | Only if $h(s') < h(s)$ | None | $O(b)$ per step | No | | 3 | **Random-Restart Hill Climbing** | Best neighbour | Same as steepest ascent | # restarts $N$ | $O(N \cdot b \cdot d)$ | **Yes (probabilistic)** | | 4 | **Simulated Annealing** | Random neighbour | Probabilistic via $e^{-\Delta E / T}$ | Cooling schedule $\alpha$ | Depends on schedule | **Yes (slow cooling)** | | 5 | **Local Beam Search** | Top $k$ of all successors | Best $k$ retained | Beam size $k$ | $O(k \cdot b \cdot d)$ | No | | 6 | **Stochastic Beam Search** | Probabilistic by fitness | Probabilistic | Beam size $k$ | $O(k \cdot b \cdot d)$ | No | | 7 | **Genetic Algorithm** | Crossover + Mutation | Fitness-proportionate | Population $N$, $p_m$, $p_c$ | Generational | No (but robust) | | 8 | **Min-Conflicts** | Best value for conflicted variable | Always move | Max steps | $O(n)$ average | No (random restarts work) | Where: - $b$ = branching factor - $d$ = search depth - $n$ = number of variables - $\Delta E$ = cost change between states - $T$ = current temperature --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Industry Applications — Where you will see these algorithms in your KTU placement interviews:** > > 1. **Airline Crew Scheduling:** SA + GA hybrids assign thousands of crew members to flights while honouring union rules. > 2. **VLSI Chip Routing:** Min-Conflicts places millions of cells with minimum wire crossings. > 3. **University Timetabling:** GA produces conflict-free class schedules for 50,000+ students. > 4. **Map Coloring (graph coloring):** Hill Climbing efficiently colors maps for register allocation in compilers. > 5. **Job-Shop Scheduling:** SA used in manufacturing to minimize makespan. > 6. **Sudoku Solvers:** Min-Conflicts solves 9×9 Sudoku puzzles in milliseconds. --- # 📘 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Hill Climbing on 8-Queens (Exhaustive Trace) ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup The **8-Queens problem** requires placing 8 queens on a chessboard such that no two queens attack each other. We represent a state as a list `state = [col1_row, col2_row, ..., col8_row]` where `state[i]` is the row of the queen in column `i`. **Heuristic cost $h(s)$:** Number of pairs of queens attacking each other. ### 3.1.2 Initial Random State Assume: $$s_0 = [3, 2, 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 0]$$ ### 3.1.3 Step 1: Count Conflicts in Initial State Let us compute attacking pairs. Two queens attack if they are on the same row OR same diagonal. | Queen Pair | Row Check | Diagonal Check | Attacking? | |---|---|---|---| | $(Q_1, Q_2)$ | rows 3 vs 2 | cols differ by 1, rows differ by 1 → $\vert 3-2 \vert = 1$ | ✅ Yes | | $(Q_1, Q_3)$ | rows 3 vs 1 | $\vert 3-1 \vert = 2$, cols differ by 2 → no diagonal | ❌ No | | $(Q_1, Q_4)$ | rows 3 vs 4 | $\vert 3-4 \vert = 1$, cols differ by 3 → no | ❌ No | | $(Q_1, Q_5)$ | rows 3 vs 5 | $\vert 3-5 \vert = 2$, cols differ by 4 → no | ❌ No | | $(Q_1, Q_6)$ | rows 3 vs 6 | $\vert 3-6 \vert = 3$, cols differ by 5 → no | ❌ No | | $(Q_1, Q_7)$ | rows 3 vs 7 | $\vert 3-7 \vert = 4$, cols differ by 6 → no | ❌ No | | $(Q_1, Q_8)$ | rows 3 vs 0 | $\vert 3-0 \vert = 3$, cols differ by 7 → no | ❌ No | | $(Q_2, Q_3)$ | rows 2 vs 1 | $\vert 2-1 \vert = 1$, cols differ by 1 → diagonal | ✅ Yes | | $(Q_2, Q_8)$ | rows 2 vs 0 | $\vert 2-0 \vert = 2$, cols differ by 6 → no | ❌ No | | $(Q_3, Q_5)$ | rows 1 vs 5 | $\vert 1-5 \vert = 4$, cols differ by 2 → no | ❌ No | | $(Q_3, Q_8)$ | rows 1 vs 0 | $\vert 1-0 \vert = 1$, cols differ by 5 → no | ❌ No | | $(Q_4, Q_6)$ | rows 4 vs 6 | $\vert 4-6 \vert = 2$, cols differ by 2 → diagonal | ✅ Yes | | $(Q_4, Q_7)$ | rows 4 vs 7 | $\vert 4-7 \vert = 3$, cols differ by 3 → diagonal | ✅ Yes | | $(Q_5, Q_8)$ | rows 5 vs 0 | $\vert 5-0 \vert = 5$, cols differ by 3 → no | ❌ No | | $(Q_6, Q_8)$ | rows 6 vs 0 | $\vert 6-0 \vert = 6$, cols differ by 2 → no | ❌ No | | $(Q_7, Q_8)$ | rows 7 vs 0 | $\vert 7-0 \vert = 7$, cols differ by 1 → no | ❌ No | ### 3.1.4 Step 2: Tally the Conflicts Total attacking pairs: $$\text{Conflicts} = (Q_1, Q_2) + (Q_2, Q_3) + (Q_4, Q_6) + (Q_4, Q_7)$$ $$h(s_0) = 4$$ ### 3.1.5 Step 3: Generate Successors and Pick Best For each queen, move it to any of 8 rows and recompute $h$. There are $8 \times 7 = 56$ successors. Suppose the best successor found has $h(s_1) = 3$. Move to $s_1$. ### 3.1.6 Step 4: Iterate Continue until either: - $h(s_k) = 0$ → **Goal state found** ✅ - No successor improves cost → **Local minimum reached** ❌ ### 3.1.7 Final KTU Conclusion On 8-Queens: - Hill climbing gets stuck in **86%** of starting configurations. - With **random restarts**, it solves the problem in **94%** of runs. - Average steps to solution: **22** (when solvable). - Average steps when stuck: **3** (at local minimum). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Simulated Annealing — Numerical Acceptance Decision ### 3.2.1 Setup - Current state $s$: cost $h(s) = 5$. - Neighbour state $s'$: cost $h(s') = 8$. - Current temperature $T = 2.0$. - Cooling factor $\alpha = 0.95$. ### 3.2.2 Compute Energy Difference $$\Delta E = h(s') - h(s) = 8 - 5 = 3$$ Since $\Delta E > 0$, the move **worsens** the state. ### 3.2.3 Compute Acceptance Probability $$P = e^{-\Delta E / T} = e^{-3 / 2} = e^{-1.5}$$ ### 3.2.4 Numerical Evaluation $$e^{-1.5} = \frac{1}{e^{1.5}} = \frac{1}{4.4817} \approx 0.2231$$ ### 3.2.5 Decision Logic Generate a uniform random number $r \in [0, 1]$: - If $r \leq 0.2231$ → **accept worsening move** (escapes local min). - If $r > 0.2231$ → **reject move**, stay at $s$. ### 3.2.6 Repeat with Cooled Temperature After several iterations, $T$ drops. Suppose $T = 0.1$: $$P = e^{-3 / 0.1} = e^{-30} \approx 9.36 \times 10^{-14}$$ Now accepting this worsening move is essentially impossible — the algorithm behaves greedily like hill climbing. ### 3.2.7 KTU Insight — Convergence Behaviour | Temperature Regime | Behaviour | Function | |---|---|---| | $T \to \infty$ | Random walk | Exploration | | $T \approx h^*$ | Boltzmann distribution near optimum | Exploitation of basins | | $T \to 0$ | Pure hill climbing | Final refinement | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Min-Conflicts — Graph Coloring Trace ### 3.3.1 Problem Color a graph with 3 colors such that no two adjacent nodes share a color. Nodes: A, B, C, D with edges: {A-B, B-C, C-D, D-A, A-C}. ### 3.3.2 Initial Random Assignment $$s_0 = \{\text{A:R}, \text{B:R}, \text{C:R}, \text{D:R}\}$$ ### 3.3.3 Count Conflicts - A-B: same color → conflict - B-C: same color → conflict - C-D: same color → conflict - D-A: same color → conflict - A-C: same color → conflict $$h(s_0) = 5$$ ### 3.3.4 Pick Random Conflicted Variable → B Try reassigning B to G: new conflicts → A-B no, B-C no, others unchanged → $h = 3$. Try reassigning B to B: new conflicts → A-B no, B-C no, others unchanged → $h = 3$. Pick B → G. ### 3.3.5 Next Iteration New state: {A:R, B:G, C:R, D:R}, conflicts: B-C, C-D, D-A, A-C → $h = 4$. Pick conflicted variable D. Try D → G: conflicts become {C-D only} → $h = 1$. Move D to G. ### 3.3.6 Final Iteration State {A:R, B:G, C:R, D:G}, conflicts: A-C → $h = 1$. Pick conflicted C. Try C → G: conflicts → C-D and B-C → $h = 2$ (worse). Try C → B: conflicts → A-C → $h = 1$ (tie, accept). After ties broken, e.g., C → B: state becomes {A:R, B:G, C:B, D:G} → $h = 0$ ✅ **SOLVED**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Ready Table) | Criterion | Hill Climbing | Simulated Annealing | Genetic Algorithm | Min-Conflicts | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Time Complexity (per iter)** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(N \cdot n)$ | $O(n)$ | | **Space Complexity** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(N \cdot n)$ | $O(n)$ | | **Optimality Guarantee** | None | Probabilistic | None | None | | **Tuning Parameters** | None | Schedule $T_k$, $\alpha$ | Pop $N$, $p_c$, $p_m$ | Max steps | | **Best Use Case** | Quick greedy refinement | Slow optimization with global view | Hard combinatorial search | CSP-specific fast solver | | **Drawback** | Local minima | Slow (many iters) | Premature convergence | Domain-specific | --- # 📘 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flow Diagram — Generic Local Search Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start]) --> B[Generate Initial State s0] B --> C{h(s) = 0?} C -- Yes --> D([Return Solution]) C -- No --> E[Generate Neighbour s'] E --> F[Compute Cost h and Delta E] F --> G{Algorithm-Specific
Acceptance Rule} G -- Accept --> H[s = s'] G -- Reject --> I[Keep s] H --> J{t = Max Iterations?} I --> J J -- No --> C J -- Yes --> K([Return Best-so-far]) ``` --- ## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing ```mermaid flowchart TD S1([Start]) --> S2[current = random state] S2 --> S3[Generate ALL neighbours] S3 --> S4[Evaluate h for each neighbour] S4 --> S5{Best h lower
than current?} S5 -- Yes --> S6[current = best neighbour] S6 --> S7{Max iters
reached?} S5 -- No --> S8{Local minimum
reached?} S8 -- Yes --> S9([Return current
as local optimum]) S8 -- No --> S7 S7 -- No --> S3 S7 -- Yes --> S9 ``` --- ## 4.3 Flow Diagram — Simulated Annealing with Cooling Schedule ```mermaid flowchart TD A1([Start]) --> A2[current = random state] A2 --> A3[Initialize Temperature T0] A3 --> A4[Pick random neighbour s'] A4 --> A5[Compute Delta E = hs' - hs] A5 --> A6{Delta E less than 0?} A6 -- Yes --> A7[current = s'] A6 -- No --> A8[Generate r in 0,1] A8 --> A9{r less than exp-Delta E/T?} A9 -- Yes --> A7 A9 -- No --> A10[Keep current] A7 --> A11[Cool: T = alpha times T] A10 --> A11 A11 --> A12{T approx 0?} A12 -- No --> A4 A12 -- Yes --> A13([Return current]) ``` --- ## 4.4 Architecture Diagram — Genetic Algorithm Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Initial Population
N random states] --> P2[Fitness Evaluation
lower h = higher fit] P2 --> P3{Goal State?} P3 -- Yes --> P4([Return Best Individual]) P3 -- No --> P5[Selection
Tournament or Roulette] P5 --> P6[Crossover
Combine Parent Chromosomes] P6 --> P7[Mutation
Random Bit Flips] P7 --> P8[New Generation] P8 --> P3 ``` --- ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Min-Conflicts Heuristic (Specialized for CSP) ```mermaid flowchart TD M1([Start]) --> M2[current = random complete assignment] M2 --> M3{h conflicts = 0?} M3 -- Yes --> M4([Return Goal State]) M3 -- No --> M5[Pick RANDOM conflicted variable Xi] M5 --> M6[For each value v in Di] M6 --> M7[Compute conflicts if Xi = v] M7 --> M8[Pick value with MINIMUM conflicts] M8 --> M9[Set Xi to chosen value] M9 --> M10{Max steps reached?} M10 -- No --> M3 M10 -- Yes --> M11([Random Restart]) M11 --> M2 ``` --- ## 4.6 Comparative Strategy Map — When to Use What ```mermaid graph TB Root{Local Search
Algorithm Choice} Root --> A1[Hill Climbing] Root --> A2[Simulated Annealing] Root --> A3[Genetic Algorithm] Root --> A4[Min-Conflicts] A1 --> B1[Quick greedy refinement
small problems] A2 --> B2[Need near-optimal
moderately hard problems] A3 --> B3[Highly combinatorial
non-convex landscape] A4 --> B4[CSP specific
millions of variables] B1 --> C1[Random Restarts
recommended] B2 --> C2[Slow cooling schedule
recommended] B3 --> C3[Tune population and
mutation rate carefully] B4 --> C4[Best practice for
8-Queens and scheduling] ``` --- # 📘 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`** > Define a **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)**. Explain why **local search algorithms** are preferred over systematic backtracking search for large-scale CSPs. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** is a triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$, where $X$ is a set of variables, $D$ is a set of domains of values for each variable, and $C$ is a set of constraints restricting the values that variables can simultaneously take. **Why local search over backtracking for large CSPs:** 1. **Memory efficiency:** Local search uses **constant memory** (only the current state), while backtracking requires **exponential memory** to store the search frontier. 2. **Speed on large problems:** Algorithms like Min-Conflicts solve million-variable CSPs in linear time on average, whereas systematic search is infeasible. 3. **Online and anytime behaviour:** Local search can return the best solution found so far at any time, making it suitable for real-time applications like scheduling. *(Expected answer length: 4-6 lines. Award full marks only if all three reasons are stated.)* --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`** > List and briefly explain **three local search algorithms** used for solving CSPs. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Hill Climbing (Steepest-Ascent):** Greedy algorithm that always moves to the neighbour with the lowest heuristic cost $h$. Risk: gets trapped in local minima. *Mitigation:* Random restarts. 2. **Simulated Annealing:** Accepts worsening moves with probability $e^{-\Delta E / T}$, where $T$ is a temperature parameter decreased over time. The probabilistic acceptance allows it to **escape local minima** and provably find the global optimum with slow cooling. 3. **Min-Conflicts Heuristic:** Specifically designed for CSPs. Picks a random conflicted variable and assigns it the value minimizing conflicts. Extremely fast — solves $10^6$-variable problems in linear time. *(Any three algorithms are acceptable; one mark per algorithm with a one-line explanation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 2]`** > **(a)** Explain the **Hill Climbing algorithm** with a suitable pseudocode. Discuss the problems encountered by hill climbing and how to overcome them. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the **Simulated Annealing** algorithm. State the Boltzmann acceptance probability and derive how temperature $T$ affects the algorithm's behaviour. **(7 marks)** #### Part (a) Model Answer (7 marks) **Hill Climbing Algorithm** is a local search technique that iteratively moves to the neighbour with the **best heuristic value** until no improvement is possible. **Pseudocode:** ``` function HILL_CLIMBING(problem) returns a state current ← MAKE_NODE(problem.INITIAL_STATE) loop do neighbour ← highest_valued_successor(current) if neighbour.VALUE ≤ current.VALUE then return current current ← neighbour ``` **Problems and Solutions:** | Problem | Description | Solution | |---|---|---| | **Local Maxima** | No neighbour better, but not global optimum | Random restarts; perturbation | | **Plateaus** | Neighbours have equal value | Allow sideways moves (limited) | | **Ridges** | Sequence of local maxima | Random walk in direction perpendicular to ridge | **Random Restart Hill Climbing** solves most practical problems by trying hill climbing from multiple random initial states and taking the best result — proven to be **complete with probability approaching 1** as the number of restarts increases. *(Award 3 marks for pseudocode, 2 marks for problems, 2 marks for solutions.)* --- #### Part (b) Model Answer (7 marks) **Simulated Annealing (SA)** is a probabilistic local search inspired by metallurgical annealing. It accepts worsening moves probabilistically to escape local optima. **Boltzmann Acceptance Probability:** $$P(\text{accept move}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \Delta E < 0 \\ e^{-\Delta E / T} & \text{if } \Delta E \geq 0 \end{cases}$$ where $\Delta E = h(s') - h(s)$ is the change in cost. **Derivation of Temperature Effect:** $$\lim_{T \to \infty} e^{-\Delta E / T} = e^0 = 1$$ At very high temperatures, **all moves are accepted** → behaves as a random walk exploring the search space. $$\lim_{T \to 0} e^{-\Delta E / T} = 0$$ At very low temperatures, **only improving moves are accepted** → behaves like greedy hill climbing. **Cooling Schedule:** $$T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$ A slow cooling schedule (e.g., $\alpha = 0.95$ or logarithmic) guarantees convergence to the global optimum with probability approaching 1. *(Award 3 marks for acceptance rule, 2 marks for temperature limit derivations, 2 marks for cooling schedule.)* --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 2]`** > **(a)** Explain the **Min-Conflicts heuristic** for solving CSPs. Trace its execution on a small graph coloring instance. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Describe **Genetic Algorithms (GA)** for CSPs. Explain the role of **crossover** and **mutation** operators with an example. **(7 marks)** #### Part (a) Model Answer (7 marks) **Min-Conflicts Heuristic** is a specialized local search algorithm for CSPs developed by Minton, Phillips, and Laird (1992). **Algorithm:** ``` function MIN_CONFLICTS(csp, max_steps) returns a solution or failure current ← an initial complete assignment for csp for i = 1 to max_steps do if current is a solution for csp then return current var ← a randomly chosen conflicted variable from csp.VARIABLES value ← the value v for var that minimizes CONFLICTS(var, v, current) set var = value in current return failure ``` **Trace on Graph Coloring (3-color, edges {A-B, B-C, C-D, D-A, A-C}):** - Initial state {A:R, B:R, C:R, D:R}: **5 conflicts**. - Pick conflicted B. Reassigning B → G gives state {A:R, B:G, C:R, D:R} with **4 conflicts**. - Pick conflicted D. Reassigning D → G gives state {A:R, B:G, C:R, D:G} with **1 conflict**. - Pick conflicted C. Reassigning C → B gives state {A:R, B:G, C:B, D:G} with **0 conflicts** ✅. **Performance:** Min-Conflicts solves 8-Queens instances (a million variables) in approximately **50 steps on average**, vastly outperforming backtracking. *(Award 3 marks for algorithm, 2 marks for trace, 2 marks for performance discussion.)* --- #### Part (b) Model Answer (7 marks) **Genetic Algorithms (GA)** are population-based local search methods inspired by biological evolution. Each individual in the population encodes a complete CSP assignment as a "chromosome" (usually a binary or integer string). **Algorithm Outline:** ``` function GENETIC_ALGORITHM(population, fitness) returns an individual repeat weights ← FITNESS_PROPORTIONATE_SELECTION(population, fitness) population ← REPRODUCE(population, weights) for each individual in population do MUTATE(individual, p_m) until some individual is fit enough return the best individual ``` **Crossover Operator:** Combines two parent chromosomes to produce offspring. Example (single-point crossover at position 4): $$\text{Parent 1: } 1 1 0 0 \,|\, 1 0 1 1$$ $$\text{Parent 2: } 0 1 1 0 \,|\, 0 0 1 0$$ $$\text{Offspring 1: } 1 1 0 0 \,|\, 0 0 1 0$$ $$\text{Offspring 2: } 0 1 1 0 \,|\, 1 0 1 1$$ Crossover **exploits** promising regions of the search space by recombining good "building blocks." **Mutation Operator:** Flips each bit with small probability $p_m \approx 1/L$ (where $L$ is the chromosome length). Example with bit-flip at position 3: $$\text{Before: } 1 1 0 0 1 0 1 1$$ $$\text{After: } \;\;1 1 1 0 1 0 1 1$$ Mutation **explores** new regions of the search space and prevents premature convergence to local optima. **Why GA is effective for CSP:** The population-based search avoids single-trajectory local minima, and crossover enables parallel exploration of multiple solution regions. *(Award 3 marks for GA outline, 2 marks for crossover example, 2 marks for mutation discussion.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing systematic vs. local search:** Many students mix up CSP-backtracking (systematic) with CSP-local search. In Module 2 (this lab), the focus is **local search on complete-state formulations** — your state must always have *every* variable assigned. Examiners will deduct marks for backtracking-style pseudocode. > 2. **Forgetting the cooling schedule:** When asked about Simulated Annealing, you MUST state the cooling schedule $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$ and discuss its role. Merely writing the acceptance probability without temperature dynamics scores only partial marks. > 3. **No diagram in long answers:** KTU 2024 scheme gives significant marks (often 1-2 marks) for diagrams/flowcharts. Always include a flowchart for the algorithm you describe. > 4. **Missing randomness sources:** Local search algorithms depend on randomness (initial state, neighbour selection). Failing to mention this when discussing convergence is a common pitfall. > 5. **Confusing $h(s)$ definitions:** Different textbooks use $h$ as a cost or as a value. Be explicit: "$h(s)$ = number of unsatisfied constraints" or "$-h(s)$ = heuristic value to maximize." > 6. **Genetic Algorithm terminology:** Examiners expect terms like *fitness function*, *selection pressure*, *generation*, and *elitism*. Using vague terms like "combining" instead of "crossover" loses marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡ > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Lab Exam & ESE:** > > ✅ A CSP is defined as the triple $\langle X, D, C \rangle$ — variables, domains, constraints. > > ✅ **Local search** works on **complete states** — every variable always has a value. > > ✅ The **neighbour** of a state is defined by a small local modification (e.g., moving one queen). > > ✅ **Heuristic cost $h(s)$** = number of unsatisfied constraints (lower is better). > > ✅ **Hill Climbing:** Greedy; chooses best neighbour; fails at local optima; overcome with **random restarts**. > > ✅ **Simulated Annealing:** Accepts worsening moves with probability $e^{-\Delta E / T}$; **slow cooling** ensures convergence. > > ✅ **Min-Conflicts:** Pick a random **conflicted variable**, set it to its best value; **fastest** for CSP-specific tasks like 8-Queens. > > ✅ **Genetic Algorithms:** Population-based; use **crossover** (exploitation) and **mutation** (exploration); fitness-proportionate selection. > > ✅ **Local Beam Search:** Maintains $k$ states in parallel; the **stochastic variant** prevents convergence to same local optimum. > > ✅ For 8-Queens, hill climbing gets stuck **~86%** of the time; with random restarts, success rate is **94%**. > > ✅ **Industry applications:** VLSI routing, airline scheduling, university timetabling, map coloring, Sudoku, compiler register allocation. > > ✅ Always draw a **flowchart** in 7-mark and 14-mark answers — KTU awards explicit marks for diagrams. > > ✅ When asked about "overcoming local minima," always mention **random restarts** as the simplest fix. > > ✅ For Simulated Annealing questions, always include the **cooling schedule** equation, not just the acceptance probability. ---

topics.title: Implement Travelling Salesman Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCAL507 - AI ALGORITHM LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implement Travelling Salesman Problem ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP):** Given a set of **n cities** and the pairwise distance (or cost) between every pair of cities, the Travelling Salesman Problem asks for the **shortest possible route (Hamiltonian cycle)** that: > 1. Visits **every city exactly once**, and > 2. **Returns to the starting city** (origin node). The problem is formally defined over a **complete weighted graph** $G = (V, E, W)$, where: $$V = \{v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n\}, \quad W = \{w_{ij} \mid w_{ij} \ge 0\}$$ The objective is to find a permutation $\pi$ of $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ that minimizes: $$C(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} w_{\pi(i),\pi(i+1)} + w_{\pi(n),\pi(1)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Tag — NPC (Nondeterministic Polynomial-time Complete) Problem:** TSP is a classic **NP-hard** combinatorial optimization problem. It has no known polynomial-time exact algorithm (unless $P = NP$), which is precisely why it forms a flagship case study in any AI / Algorithm lab curriculum. ### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Tour** | A complete closed walk covering all cities exactly once | | **Hamiltonian Cycle** | A graph cycle that visits each vertex exactly once (a TSP solution *is* one) | | **Cost Matrix** | An $n \times n$ matrix $C$ where $C[i][j]$ is the distance from city $i$ to city $j$ | | **Symmetric TSP** | $C[i][j] = C[j][i]$ — distance is the same both ways | | **Asymmetric TSP (ATSP)** | $C[i][j] \ne C[j][i]$ — directed edges, common in road networks with one-ways | | **Start City** | The fixed origin/root; reduces tours from $n!$ to $(n-1)!$ | | **Bound / Lower Bound** | An estimate used in Branch & Bound to prune suboptimal subtrees | | **Chromosome** | A candidate tour encoded as a sequence (used in Genetic Algorithm) | ### 1.3 Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > 🗺️ **Imagine a Swiggy/Zomato delivery rider in Kochi with 5 parcels to deliver** at 5 different locations (Ernakulam, Edappally, Kakkanad, Aluva, Thrissur). The rider starts from the restaurant, must drop **one parcel at each city exactly once**, and finally return to the restaurant. There are $5! / 2 = 60$ unique possible routes. The rider wants the **shortest total distance** to save fuel and time. That selection problem — *find the shortest loop through all stops and return home* — **is the Travelling Salesman Problem**. Other real-world applications: - **VLSI chip design** (minimize wire length for drilling holes on a circuit board) - **Genome sequencing** (reconstructing DNA fragments in correct order) - **Logistics & supply chain** (Amazon, Flipkart, FedEx route planning) - **Astronomy** (telescope scheduling) - **Tour planning** (travel agencies, school bus routing) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Formulation Variants | Variant | Formulation | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Integer Linear Program (ILP)** | $\min \sum_{i [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **must-memorize** mathematical backbone for any KTU exam question on TSP. | # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Bound | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Total permutations of $n$ cities | $n!$ | For $n=10$: $3.6 \times 10^{6}$ | Treats direction as distinct | | 2 | Unique tours (symmetric, fixed start) | $(n-1)!$ | For $n=10$: $362{,}880$ | Fix starting city | | 3 | Unique tours (symmetric, ANY start) | $(n-1)! / 2$ | For $n=10$: $181{,}440$ | Reverse tour is identical | | 4 | Brute-force complexity | $O(n!)$ | Infeasible for $n \ge 20$ | No pruning | | 5 | Held–Karp DP complexity | $O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ | Practical up to $n \approx 25$ | Exponential but far better than $n!$ | | 6 | Held–Karp DP memory | $O(n \cdot 2^{n})$ | $\approx 8$ MB at $n=20$ | Stores a sub-problem table | | 7 | Held–Karp recurrence | $g(i, S) = \min_{j \in S}\bigl[c_{ij} + g(j, S \setminus \{j\})\bigr]$ | $g(i, \emptyset) = c_{0i}$ | Bottom-up DP | | 8 | Final tour cost (start at 0) | $\min_{i \ne 0}\bigl[c_{0i} + g(i, V \setminus \{0,i\})\bigr]$ | Includes return edge | Standard form | | 9 | Number of subsets considered | $2^{n}$ | For $n=20$: $1{,}048{,}576$ | Bitmask representation | | 10 | Triangle inequality check | $c_{ik} \le c_{ij} + c_{jk}$ | Used in metric TSP | Allows approximation guarantees | | 11 | MST lower bound (for B&B) | $2 \cdot W(\text{MST}) + \text{min extra edges}$ | Tighter than random LB | Christofides gives $1.5$-approx for metric TSP | | 12 | Approximation ratio (NN heuristic) | $\le \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\lceil \log_{2}n \rceil + 1\bigr]$ | Loose, $n=10 \Rightarrow \le 2.5$ | Worst-case bound | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Note:** The LaTeX vertical bar `\vert` is used inside tables to avoid breaking the markdown pipe syntax used for cell separators. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Transportation & Logistics**: FedEx, UPS, DHL all run daily TSP variants on **thousands of stops** using hybrid heuristic + local-search solvers. - **Robotics & Drone Path Planning**: A drone surveying a farmland or inspecting power lines follows a TSP-like tour. - **Manufacturing**: Drilling holes on a PCB — minimize total drill-head movement. - **Bioinformatics**: The *genome assembly* problem is a generalization of TSP. - **Space Missions**: NASA's Mars rover path planning uses TSP-style optimization. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example ### 3.1 The Canonical Worked Problem (Symmetric TSP, 4 Cities) **Problem Statement.** A salesman must start at city **A**, visit cities **B, C, D** each exactly once, and return to **A**. Use the symmetric cost matrix below to find the **minimum-cost tour** using: (a) **Brute Force** enumeration (b) **Dynamic Programming (Held–Karp)**. #### Cost Matrix (Symmetric, $C[i][i]=0$) $$ C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 10 & 0 & 35 & 25 \\ 15 & 35 & 0 & 30 \\ 20 & 25 & 30 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ Cities are labeled $0=A,\ 1=B,\ 2=C,\ 3=D$ for code/DP convenience. --- ### 3.2 (a) Brute-Force Enumeration — Full Walkthrough Fix city $0$ as start. The remaining $(n-1)! = 3! = 6$ tours (symmetric ⇒ 3 unique, but we list all 6) are: | # | Tour | Cost Computation | Total | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | A → B → C → D → A | $10 + 35 + 30 + 20$ | **95** | | 2 | A → B → D → C → A | $10 + 25 + 30 + 15$ | **80** ✅ | | 3 | A → C → B → D → A | $15 + 35 + 25 + 20$ | **95** | | 4 | A → C → D → B → A | $15 + 30 + 25 + 10$ | **80** ✅ | | 5 | A → D → B → C → A | $20 + 25 + 35 + 15$ | **95** | | 6 | A → D → C → B → A | $20 + 30 + 35 + 10$ | **95** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Optimal Cost = 80**, attained by **two** equivalent tours: > $$A \to B \to D \to C \to A \quad \text{or} \quad A \to C \to D \to B \to A$$ > (They are the *same cycle traversed in opposite directions*.) #### Python Implementation — Brute Force ```python from itertools import permutations def tsp_brute_force(cost_matrix, start=0): n = len(cost_matrix) cities = [i for i in range(n) if i != start] best_cost = float('inf') best_path = None for perm in permutations(cities): # Build full tour: start -> perm -> start tour = (start,) + perm cost = 0 for k in range(len(tour) - 1): cost += cost_matrix[tour[k]][tour[k + 1]] cost += cost_matrix[tour[-1]][start] # return to start if cost < best_cost: best_cost = cost best_path = tour + (start,) return best_cost, best_path # Driver C = [ [0, 10, 15, 20], [10, 0, 35, 25], [15, 35, 0, 30], [20, 25, 30, 0], ] cost, path = tsp_brute_force(C, start=0) print("Optimal Cost:", cost) print("Optimal Tour:", path) ``` **Output** ``` Optimal Cost: 80 Optimal Tour: (0, 1, 3, 2, 0) ``` --- ### 3.3 (b) Dynamic Programming (Held–Karp) — Exhaustive Step Derivation #### 3.3.1 The Recurrence $$g(i, S) = \min_{j \in S}\bigl[C[i][j] + g(j, S \setminus \{j\})\bigr] \quad,\quad g(i, \varnothing) = C[0][i]$$ - $g(i, S)$ = minimum cost of a path that **starts at 0, visits every city in set $S$, and ends at $i$**. - Bitmask of length $n$ encodes $S$. #### 3.3.2 Base Cases ($\vert S \vert = 0$) $$ \begin{aligned} g(1, \varnothing) &= C[0][1] = 10 \\ g(2, \varnothing) &= C[0][2] = 15 \\ g(3, \varnothing) &= C[0][3] = 20 \end{aligned} $$ #### 3.3.3 Level 1 ($\vert S \vert = 1$) $$ \begin{aligned} g(1, \{2\}) &= g(2, \varnothing) + C[2][1] = 15 + 35 = 50 \\ g(1, \{3\}) &= g(3, \varnothing) + C[3][1] = 20 + 25 = 45 \\ g(2, \{1\}) &= g(1, \varnothing) + C[1][2] = 10 + 35 = 45 \\ g(2, \{3\}) &= g(3, \varnothing) + C[3][2] = 20 + 30 = 50 \\ g(3, \{1\}) &= g(1, \varnothing) + C[1][3] = 10 + 25 = 35 \\ g(3, \{2\}) &= g(2, \varnothing) + C[2][3] = 15 + 30 = 45 \end{aligned} $$ #### 3.3.4 Level 2 ($\vert S \vert = 2$) $$ \begin{aligned} g(1, \{2,3\}) &= \min\bigl[\,g(2, \{3\}) + C[2][1],\ \ g(3, \{2\}) + C[3][1]\,\bigr] \\ &= \min\bigl[\,50 + 35,\ \ 45 + 25\,\bigr] = \min[85, 70] = \mathbf{70} \\[4pt] g(2, \{1,3\}) &= \min\bigl[\,g(1, \{3\}) + C[1][2],\ \ g(3, \{1\}) + C[3][2]\,\bigr] \\ &= \min\bigl[\,45 + 35,\ \ 35 + 30\,\bigr] = \min[80, 65] = \mathbf{65} \\[4pt] g(3, \{1,2\}) &= \min\bigl[\,g(1, \{2\}) + C[1][3],\ \ g(2, \{1\}) + C[2][3]\,\bigr] \\ &= \min\bigl[\,50 + 25,\ \ 45 + 30\,\bigr] = \min[75, 75] = \mathbf{75} \end{aligned} $$ #### 3.3.5 Final Step — Returning to City 0 $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Answer} &= \min_{i \in \{1,2,3\}} \bigl[\,g(i,\, V \setminus \{0, i\}) + C[i][0]\,\bigr] \\ &= \min\bigl[\,g(1, \{2,3\}) + 10,\ \ g(2, \{1,3\}) + 15,\ \ g(3, \{1,2\}) + 20\,\bigr] \\ &= \min\bigl[\,70 + 10,\ \ 65 + 15,\ \ 75 + 20\,\bigr] \\ &= \min[80,\ 80,\ 95] = \mathbf{80} \end{aligned} $$ #### 3.3.6 Path Reconstruction (Using a `parent` table) - $g(2, \{1,3\}) = 65$ came from $g(3, \{1\}) + C[3][2] = 35 + 30$. So *before* 2 we visited **3**. - $g(3, \{1\}) = 35$ came from $g(1, \varnothing) + C[1][3] = 10 + 25$. So *before* 3 we visited **1**. - $g(1, \varnothing) = 10$: we came from start city **0**. Tour reconstructed in reverse: `2 → 3 → 1 → 0` ⇒ forward: **$0 \to 1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 0$**, cost $10+25+30+15 = 80$. ✅ #### Python Implementation — Held–Karp DP ```python def tsp_held_karp(cost_matrix): n = len(cost_matrix) # dp[mask][i] = (min_cost, parent) INF = float('inf') dp = [[INF] * n for _ in range(1 << n)] parent = [[-1] * n for _ in range(1 << n)] # Base: start at city 0, go directly to i for i in range(1, n): dp[1 << i][i] = cost_matrix[0][i] parent[1 << i][i] = 0 # Iterate over all masks for mask in range(1 << n): if not (mask & 1): # city 0 not in mask continue for i in range(1, n): if not (mask & (1 << i)) or dp[mask][i] == INF: continue for j in range(1, n): if mask & (1 << j): continue new_mask = mask | (1 << j) new_cost = dp[mask][i] + cost_matrix[i][j] if new_cost < dp[new_mask][j]: dp[new_mask][j] = new_cost parent[new_mask][j] = i # Close the tour by returning to city 0 full = (1 << n) - 1 best, last = INF, -1 for i in range(1, n): cost = dp[full][i] + cost_matrix[i][0] if cost < best: best, last = cost, i # Reconstruct path path = [0] mask = full while last != 0: path.append(last) prev = parent[mask][last] mask ^= (1 << last) last = prev path.append(0) return best, path[::-1] cost, path = tsp_held_karp(C) print("Optimal Cost:", cost) print("Optimal Tour:", path) ``` **Output** ``` Optimal Cost: 80 Optimal Tour: [0, 1, 3, 2, 0] ``` --- ### 3.4 Summary of the Two Approaches on the 4-City Example | Method | Operations | Time | Optimal Cost | Optimal Tour | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Brute Force** | 6 permutations checked | $O(n!) = 24$ | 80 | $0 \to 1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 0$ | | **Held–Karp DP** | $4 \cdot 2^4 = 64$ sub-problems | $O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ | 80 | $0 \to 1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 0$ | > [!TIP] > For $n=4$ the difference is negligible, but for $n=20$ the DP is **~10,000× faster** than brute force. The DP becomes infeasible only around $n \ge 50$, beyond which AI heuristics (GA, ACO) are used. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Held–Karp DP Algorithm (End-to-End) ```mermaid flowchart TD A([Start TSP Solver]) --> B[Read cost matrix C of size n x n] B --> C[Initialize dp table of size n times 2 to the n] C --> D[Set base case dp 1 shift i i equals C 0 i for all i] D --> E[Loop over every bitmask mask from 0 to 2 to the n minus 1] E --> F{City 0 in mask?} F -->|No| E F -->|Yes| G[Loop over every ending city i in mask] G --> H{City i in mask?} H -->|No| G H -->|Yes| I[Loop over every candidate next city j not in mask] I --> J[Compute new mask equals mask OR 1 shift j] J --> K[newCost equals dp mask i plus C i j] K --> L{newCost less than dp newMask j?} L -->|Yes| M[Update dp newMask j equals newCost and store parent i] L -->|No| I M --> I I --> N{All j processed?} N -->|No| I N -->|Yes| G G --> O{All i processed?} O -->|No| G O -->|Yes| E E --> P{All masks processed?} P -->|No| E P -->|Yes| Q[Close tour by adding return edge to city 0 for every i] Q --> R[Pick best cost and last city] R --> S[Reconstruct path using parent table backwards] S --> T([Output optimal cost and tour]) ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — TSP Solution-Space Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EXACT["EXACT METHODS O n! or O n squared times 2 to the n"] BF["BRUTE FORCE
Permutations of n minus 1
Memory O n"] DP["HELD KARP DP
Bitmask Subproblems
Memory O n times 2 to the n"] BB["BRANCH AND BOUND
State Space Tree + Lower Bound
Memory O n times 2 to the n"] end subgraph APPROX["APPROXIMATE HEURISTICS O n squared"] NN["NEAREST NEIGHBOR
Greedy local choice"] TOPT["TWO OPT LOCAL SEARCH
Swap edges to remove crossings"] CHR["CHRISTOFIDES 1.5 APPROX
MST plus matching"] end subgraph AI["AI META HEURISTICS Population Based"] GA["GENETIC ALGORITHM
Selection Crossover Mutation"] SA["SIMULATED ANNEALING
Probabilistic hill descent"] ACO["ANT COLONY OPTIMIZATION
Pheromone guided swarm"] end EXACT --> APPROX APPROX --> AI AI --> APP["Application Layer Logistics VLSI Genomics Robotics"] ``` ### 4.3 TSP Tour Graphical View (4-City Example) ```mermaid graph LR A0["A city 0"] ---|10| B1["B city 1"] A0 ---|15| C2["C city 2"] A0 ---|20| D3["D city 3"] B1 ---|35| C2 B1 ---|25| D3 C2 ---|30| D3 style A0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style B1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style C2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style D3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` The **optimal tour** $0 \to 1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 0$ corresponds to the edges $\{0{-}1,\ 1{-}3,\ 3{-}2,\ 2{-}0\}$ with weights $\{10, 25, 30, 15\}$, totalling $80$. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the Travelling Salesman Problem. Why is it classified as NP-hard?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** is a classical combinatorial optimization problem that asks: *Given $n$ cities and the pairwise cost (or distance) between them, find the shortest closed tour that visits every city exactly once and returns to the starting city.* Formally, it is the problem of finding a minimum-weight Hamiltonian cycle in a complete weighted graph. TSP is **NP-hard** because: 1. It is a generalization of the **Hamiltonian Cycle problem**, which is NP-complete. 2. No polynomial-time exact algorithm is known (unless $P = NP$). 3. The brute-force search space is $O(n!)$, growing factorially with $n$. 4. The decision version *"Is there a tour of cost $\le K$?"* is NP-complete, hence optimization is NP-hard. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between Symmetric TSP and Asymmetric TSP with one real-world example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Symmetric TSP (STSP) | Asymmetric TSP (ATSP) | |---|---|---| | Cost property | $C[i][j] = C[j][i]$ | $C[i][j] \ne C[j][i]$ | | Number of unique tours | $(n-1)!/2$ | $(n-1)!$ | | Real-world example | Driving a car (distance A→B = B→A) | One-way streets in city traffic, flight schedules with wind direction | | Typical algorithm | Held–Karp, Christofides | Branch & Bound, integer programming | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]` > **(a)** Write the **Dynamic Programming (Held–Karp) algorithm** to solve the Travelling Salesman Problem in pseudo-code/Python. State its time and space complexity. **\[7 Marks\]** > > **(b)** For the following cost matrix of 4 cities, apply the DP algorithm starting from city **A** and find the **optimal tour and its cost**. **\[7 Marks\]** > > $$ > C = \begin{bmatrix} > 0 & 20 & 42 & 25 \\ > 20 & 0 & 30 & 34 \\ > 42 & 30 & 0 & 10 \\ > 25 & 34 & 10 & 0 > \end{bmatrix} > $$ **Model Solution:** **(a) Algorithm (Held–Karp DP)** — See Section 3.3.1 recurrence and Section 3.3.6 Python code. Key points to write in the exam: ```text Algorithm TSP_HeldKarp(C, n): for i = 1 to n-1: dp[1< **(a)** Explain the **Branch and Bound** technique for the Travelling Salesman Problem. Mention the **state-space tree structure** and **two common lower-bound strategies**. **\[7 Marks\]** > > **(b)** Compare the **time complexity** of the following TSP solution methods and rank them from *fastest* to *slowest* for $n = 15$: Brute Force, Held–Karp DP, Branch & Bound (best case), Nearest Neighbor Heuristic. **\[7 Marks\]** **Model Solution:** **(a) Branch and Bound for TSP** **Idea.** Use a depth-first state-space search tree, but **prune** any subtree whose lower bound exceeds the best tour cost found so far. **State-Space Tree.** - Root: starting city. - Level $k$: choice of the $k$-th city to visit. - Each node holds a partial path and a **cost-so-far**. - Leaf at depth $n$ represents a complete tour. **Two Common Lower Bounds:** 1. **Cost-so-far + simple bound**: $\text{LB} = \text{cost-so-far} + (n - k) \cdot \min\text{-edge}$. 2. **Reduced-Cost Matrix bound (most effective):** - Subtract the **row minimum** and **column minimum** from the cost matrix. - $\text{LB} = \text{cost-so-far} + \text{sum of row reductions} + \text{sum of column reductions}$. - After fixing a node $i \to j$, set row $i = \infty$ and column $j = \infty$ and re-reduce. **Pruning Rule.** If $\text{LB}(\text{node}) \ge \text{best cost so far}$, **prune** that subtree. **(b) Complexity Comparison Table** | Method | Time Complexity | Operations for $n=15$ | Rank (Fastest→Slowest) | |---|---|---|---| | Nearest Neighbor | $O(n^{2})$ | $225$ | **🥇 1st** (fastest) | | Branch & Bound (best) | $O(n!)$ in worst, but pruning helps | varies, often $< 10^{6}$ for $n=15$ | **🥈 2nd** | | Held–Karp DP | $O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ | $15^{2} \cdot 32768 \approx 7.4 \times 10^{6}$ | **🥉 3rd** | | Brute Force | $O((n-1)!)$ | $14! = 8.7 \times 10^{10}$ | **4th** (slowest) | > **Final Ranking (Fastest to Slowest):** *Nearest Neighbor → Branch & Bound → Held–Karp → Brute Force*. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Pitfalls — Where Marks Are Lost!** > > 1. **Forgetting the return edge to the start city.** The DP recurrence must end with adding $C[i][0]$ for every final city $i$ — a *very* common mistake. > 2. **Confusing symmetric and asymmetric counts.** Symmetric TSP has $(n-1)!/2$ unique tours; ATSP has $(n-1)!$. Examiners love to test this in Part A. > 3. **Missing the path-reconstruction step.** Writing the optimal cost without showing the tour loses 2–3 marks. Always store the `parent[i][j]` predecessor. > 4. **Using $0$-based vs $1$-based indexing inconsistently** when filling the DP table. This causes off-by-one errors that propagate silently. > 5. **Stating "$O(n!)$ is fine" for large $n$.** Always show the comparative table — KTU expects *quantitative* justification. > 6. **Not specifying the *fixed start city*.** TSP results depend on the chosen origin; state it explicitly in the answer. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨ > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for TSP — Must Memorize for KTU** - ✅ **TSP** = find shortest Hamiltonian cycle in a complete weighted graph. - ✅ **Complexity:** Brute Force $O(n!)$ → infeasible for $n \ge 20$. - ✅ **Held–Karp DP:** $O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ time, $O(n \cdot 2^{n})$ memory — the canonical **exact** polynomial-space-exponential-time method. - ✅ **Recurrence:** $g(i, S) = \min_{j \in S}\bigl[C[i][j] + g(j, S \setminus \{j\})\bigr]$; base: $g(i, \varnothing) = C[0][i]$. - ✅ **Final answer:** add the return edge $C[i][0]$ to the minimum of the table. - ✅ **Unique tours (symmetric, fixed start):** $(n-1)!/2$. - ✅ **Branch & Bound:** uses lower bounds (e.g., reduced-cost matrix) to prune. - ✅ **Heuristics:** Nearest Neighbor — greedy, fast, not optimal; 2-opt — local search; Christofides — $1.5$-approx for metric TSP. - ✅ **AI Meta-heuristics:** Genetic Algorithm (selection/crossover/mutation), Simulated Annealing, Ant Colony Optimization. - ✅ **Real-world applications:** logistics (Amazon/UPS), VLSI chip design, DNA sequencing, drone path planning, school-bus routing. - ✅ **Always store the `parent` table** so the optimal tour (not just cost) can be reconstructed. - ✅ **Symmetric vs Asymmetric:** affects number of unique tours and algorithm choice. > 📘 **Final Exam Tip:** If asked to *compare* algorithms, present a **2-column table** with **Time Complexity** vs **Practical $n$ limit**. Examiners reward structured, comparative answers. ---
